speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Gustav Carlson, who is better known as Bud Carlson and who is the son of Joseph Carlson of Burnt Ridge, took place at his nephew's home near Troy on July 11th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And he was born on a farm out there. And of course they knew that they realized these farms didn't support that many people. They had to go elsewhere to work. Some few of his, he was a family of 11 children. He came in a family of 11 children. He was the 11th. Eight of those things eventually migrated to America. Four before he did. Four of the older ones. And one brother came with him. And then two sisters followed at later intervals. So there were eight that spent most of their lives here. Well the last one didn't come right away. Most of her children had migrated first. And then finally, because she was just too young, this one came over and was joined. But his parents didn't come along. No, no. Well his mother was dead almost so he barely could remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How bad was it in Sweden at that time for people?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well it was hard work having not easy living. I guess nobody ever starved. Really that is. ... was never complete in Sweden. It looked like even in those days. And improved gradually since. Well it's quite a different country now. And has been for a long time. I visited there in 1928 the first time. Oh and again in 1961. Of course I can see a tremendous difference in 33 years. But then they were pretty well. A lot of things were done. They had good roads in. And they had lots of cars in 28.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did your father and mother wind up coming to Moscow?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well he was worked at in Minnesota. He went into Carpentry. And he worked at that. And he came first to Spokane. He had a job waiting for him in Spokane. He hired by a lumber company that was centered in Minneapolis. They were building out there. And they were going to build a mill. And he went out there to build that mill. The house of machinery. And put it in when it arrived. Building a saw mill. And he had a partner in that that took a contract to build a, put up the buildings. And put in the machinery when it came. And he continued working there for some time. That was in 1887 I think he came out there yes. 1887. Actually he went out there a few months before Mother and managed to find a piece of land in which he could buy. And worked in intervals when he was working on that on his own time. Building a house on it. So she came in summer. My brother Paul was born in the fall of that year. Must of I used to third in the family. She had already had two children born in Minnesota. But around 1891 they had some depression years there. Panics they used to call it. And it wasn't so good. And work was getting scarcer. And he came down to Moscow and that was the steel company. The construction company was building the first university building. In Moscow it was a wooden structure. So he came down to work on that. He was building foreman on that. On the erection of that first university there. Oh quite a few years later that thing burned down some other. I don't know what year that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This the administration building?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well they just put up a single building in 1891. No it was wooden. Wooden building they put up first. The next building that went up was the brick I think. I know the first buildings I ever saw were brick and cement. And I was old enough to see what they were. So then he worked carpentry on the first building. The university building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then how did he wind up deciding to become a farmer?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well he gathered together a little money. He came off of a farm. And he had the land idea. That's the land. They were taking up land and buying land. They'd be homesteaders here quite a few and some of the homesteaders were selling out. And that was the land that had been homesteaded out there. And the fellow who sold out wanted to sell out and go over where his relatives, brothers, he had a lot of brothers. He was found running into some briskles. Well this was Jack Crystal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a, oh I guess a young Jack Crystal died a few years ago did you know him?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I knew of him. Oh well his widow's know him. And his son is Dennis Crystal. Right. Well he was an uncle of just Jack Crystal. Old Jack he remained unmarried. But I think he gave his land to his brother Joe's sons. But then he wanted to move over to Driscoll Ridge. Yes he bought in over there on Driscoll Ridge. And he had his relatives there. The Criscles and the Dunes and the Cunninghams were all his relatives. And he came originally from Eastern Canada and settled in there. So your father bought that land out there. Well he worked at carpentry around Moscow particularly building houses in the city and in the country around for about until 1899. He was working at that. And then he bought that land out there. Oh he continued to work. He worked for a time after he got that to get runny cash.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Goodies. That's exciting… because there were new houses going out. Some of the old cabins were being taken down and new cabins. He built a house where Clarence Johnson lived. There was a fellow named Liverson who lived there at that time. And he wanted that new house built. That was one of them. And that house where Rudolph Smith lived.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it Liverson Smith?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No. Yeah. Now I basically remember growing up there. They came in the, what they did, they came in 1902. The kids came in the house but I think Pearson had been up there before. I don't know which year that was erected. But I think it was the summer before they started building on it. I think he wasn't satisfied with the little house. He had a big family of about nine children. Well they didn't have nine when he came. I guess they had eight. And there was a little old house on that place. It took hardly households. So he had, I believe he completed that house before he moved his family up. He lived down in Genesee. And he continued to own a farm down there after he moved up here. So your father made, could make some money building houses before he was starting farming. Yes. You see I don't think there was more than 30 acres broke on that place. And we got out there. And there was the amount of land on top of the hill before it tipped into the canyon. The following, it was 160 acres and a mile long and a quarter wide. It was about 90 acres. We broke up some rather flat ground down in the canyon where it had leveled off. We were about 10 acres more later on. We were busy breaking out that clearing of trees and the brush and cutting wood. And we sold wood as we did it and did a piecemeal there. I think it was about 1995. So 1899 and 1995, we got it all cleared out on top of the original farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did the kids help?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I would say quite a bit. My two oldest brothers were mostly in command of farming. He was out building a good deal during those years. He built several houses on Burnt Ridge and some elsewhere in this county. I never did make her think particularly about it. I remember him sometimes years later, who'd drive by the car, I built this house. Talking about the kids helping the family. My two oldest brothers, Paulter and Paul, the water was 15, well, came 16 the next spring. He took the brunt of the work around there. Paul was about three years younger, but Paul was always strong in Husky as a kid. Four years later, he had his troubles, but he didn't get an awful lot of the work. You see, there wasn't too much. He didn't go to school too much. He went to school out of there, but school terms were not like they are now. They didn't go to school nine months in a year. They went about five months in country schools. A couple of months, maybe, in the colder weather in the fall, and he generally laid off during the instance of the winter season and went about two or three months in the spring. They extended that to about, I don't think they ever got up to seven months, those years in which they went to school. Actually, the first school year I spent was supposed to be a seven-month school, but it wasn't.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: The teacher left for some reason. At the end of about five or six months, I think. That would have been in 1905. That was the end of the school year. That was the school year, and they never went beyond seven months. They had good seven months the last two or three years. About five years, I guess, that I was in school. One year, they had a divided term. I remember my sister, Edith, was in school there, and she was just older. She really ran and was supposed to run four months in the fall and three months in the spring, but I can't understand how they did that, because they were out too early to have had it laid off. They could put that months in between. I think it was less than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of school when you first went there?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I kind of enjoyed it when first.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember it being like?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I'll tell you, they had pretty fair teachers there, I suppose, and they rattled the whole board. Poor the kids there at the 40, and they sent those two out. Sometimes, I don't know, I don't think that average is probably less than that… whatever… oh, I can remember. The foundation didn't have high qualifications, but education. That I know. I don't know what the first two teachers I had had, or three. Well, the third teacher, he remained in education in this area, and he became a university graduate by going back to school at the time, and was a superintendent of a school here in this county. I think it was a bit potlatch before he retired. He was trying to retire, and he just kept on for a long, long time. But he took his education piecemeal while he taught in the meantime. He was married already.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was school like for you as a youngster going there? Could you learn as much as you were ready for?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I don't know whether it was or not. They all tried to allow a certain amount of time for all the kids to get them started. I don't know if they had any kid out there who was retarded enough that he couldn't learn his to read with a fairly short time. They were at least all that right. They didn't have any real nominees to continue with, I'd say. Nothing particularly brilliant, I suppose, about most of us. But nobody was on the weak side. There were some that became sadly retarded, but the thing is, the boys so often laid out a school in the fall and the spring to help their parents with the work. So they spent such a too small time to get consecutive education in. There were gaps. They'd come back and enter classes with other children. They couldn't make special classes for them. They'd just get in, and they'd sometimes be in the same grade twice, or they'd look ready for it to the teacher while he moved up to something else.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: That held true almost up to the end of my school experience out there, except that last year, the state passed more stringent school laws, qualifications of teachers and for attendance, and compelled them to come back unless they were... They made it first 18 years old before they could stop. That was later, lower to 16, because they thought they had given not much use if they were 16, and compelled them to come back. But a whole bunch of them came back that winter fairly early. They went to school. Well, would you say that most of those big boys started laying out when they were 12 or 12 years old a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That happened in your family?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, it more or less happened with Paul and Walter. Paul had completed the sixth grade, and Walter was to be in the eighth. Actually, Ellen told me that she and Walter had been recommended by the teachers in the school. They were to take the entrance examinations for the university preparatory school. They had a university preparatory high school there. For that fall, when they came out, but they never entered because we moved out here. That was one thing, too. They hadn't been able to get a teacher that first year. That's what schools would make until spring. A burnt ranch, a burnt ranch. And then they got a girl to teach for a few months in the spring. So then the kids just didn't go to school for that particular year. The fall of 1899, they didn't go to school. The wages were tremendously low for teachers in the school. It was $30 a month or something like that. But they got this girl from Little Bear Ridge. I think she'd been down to Lewiston, or had school in. Or maybe she'd been just to high school, or maybe not even that, because they had what they called first, second, and third grade certificates. And they could teach on the third grade certificate if they passed an examination in school subjects.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And that's where some of them weren't even high school graduates that were taught. They were none young, but they passed a test in. Now, Joe said, my brother Joe told me later, he went into education and spent his life in it. That he saw, they had a point to find out about those examinations, and he saw some old lists of them. And he said they gave them some hard examinations. So very hard examinations on school subjects, so that if a person passed those tests, it showed he knew something about it. He'd probably a whole lot more than he'd learned in school. So in that respect, he generally got people who were probably quite intelligent.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that most of the teachers in the school, like the Burnt Ridge School and these other ridge schools, were capable teachers?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think they did fairly good. Nearly all of them could get the children started. The primary kids started, they knew how to do that, whether they'd been trained in it or not. And they taught things there. There were one or two that were kind of weak, I think. My brother Carl, who was there when he was 14. He said that that teacher they had that year was stuck on hard problems in the Besides that, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they strict teachers?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Some were fairly strict. Oh, not. Well, I remember Mr. Peterson. That was the man I said that taught early there. I had him for four months one year. He was quite strict. He had a revocation. And he's one state I said went on in education and got himself a good education before he quit going back to school. He was, but he was the only woman teacher I ever had out there. She was strict.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of the teachers you had were men?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Men, all except to... Excuse me. I'm going to run down to Nola's a minute. Just use anything you want in the house and I'll be back later. That was the only woman teacher I had. I had her for only about two months. I kind of had the idea that most of these schools, it was mostly women teaching. Well, I think there were lots of women teachers, but somehow on Burnt Ridge they got men teachers. But there were quite a few men, and I like... Well, there is the Sealsmiths here. Their father, Frank Sealsmith, taught school two years on Burnt Ridge. And he did it on a third grade certificate. Which means he just had to pass an examination. I heard he was a bit of a mean teacher. Well, he was, I think in a way, he wasn't particularly strict. But he was hard on some kids, easy on others. I really believe that. I don't think, maybe he thought that he didn't like some kids.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Or something. Ted Sandell hated his guts. I don't know if you know Ted. Well, he hated him like sin. But he did something to Ted there. They didn't match. Ted was all just fooling around, chewing up paper. As if it was gum and tossing God on his desk. He came over there, and he just started picking up paper. He says, chew it, chew it, chew it. He put a sheet off of his tablet, and I opened it up and said, chew it. So he had his mouth full, and he got a good darnedly wound. Ted was about ready to re-mail. But he didn't. Ted was a big kid then. He would have been difficult for Stegelsmith to handle. He decided to re-mail. But he was so mad that he hated, I think he hated Stegelsmith in that day. Well, it was a funny kind of punishment. Yeah, it really was. It was an odd kind of punishment for being floppy, and attracting attention to the other kids. And doing it for that purpose. It wasn't an odd sort of thing to do. He had to let the paper in his mouth, and he was near to choking. Yeah, I can see why I wouldn't like him after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you know, you talked about the kids, the boys, laying out of school, and helping on the places, and it makes me wonder, do you think that that held, well, would wind up holding some of the boys back?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, yes, it held them back. I've been, as they are now, those kids have all gone on to high school probably, every one of them. But in those days, the people didn't think of such things as high school. They thought that was a higher education that was beyond the grasp of simple farmers, and farmer's kids. They had a certain respect for it, but they thought it took huge talent. It was a thing for the very brilliant. I suppose that a few of them would think about college, if they felt that way, by high school. Yes, very few. The first kid to go away to go to school is my brother Joe. Peterson came down and told my dads that Joe wouldn't profit by any more here. He said that he's by sending him away because he's a good scholar. And that year, they had the, at Lewiston Normal School, as it was called, they had a preparatory, and a couple of years extra rolled into one. And he went down there to go to school. And later, Carl went. Those were the first two that left the ridge. After we got started, we kept on going to school. Well, your father didn't mind losing their work. Oh, no, no. No, he believed in education.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: He was sorry that he hadn't had more. No, our parents were strong for education. And they weren't in a position to send everybody from the beginning. Their mother actually was rather reluctant to leave Moscow because she just thought that they had good schools there. And she was really put out when they came out on the farm that year and found that they hadn't even gotten a school teacher for that fall. Then, too, I could imagine that a lot of women, I don't know how your mother felt about it, but for some women to live out on the farm on the ridge would be a lot rougher than living in town. Yes, it was. Farm women worked hard out there. Most of the grandchildren, too, in those days. And they didn't have the conveniences. No electricity. No cars. And none of that was... As far as going to town is concerned, we seldom went to town any more often than we need to lay in groceries. They often raised a few cattle and canned or smoked the beet for the off-season. And then bought some fresh in the big cattle and hogs nearly everybody, chickens.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And they raised vegetable gardens. And all that took time and work. All that. All the chores around the farm. It was long days that farmers put in, actually. There were no eight-hour days there. We were near 14 when they got up in the warings when they were ready to go to the... More than that, when they got caught in the meal times then, before they were ready to go to the vet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean the way it sounds, what you're saying, is that there really wasn't any leisure kind of at all?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: There wasn't any leisure during the day, really. You went up in the morning and started taking care of the animals. And harnessing horses and getting ready for the fields. A large part of the time, spring and fall. Even in the summer, there was hay and culled eggs and things of that sort. And then you had breakfast. After a few days, taking care of your animals, then went to the fields. At noon, you'd have a little leisure time. Usually you had to rest your horses enough so they could stand to work, too. And then on to supper time and then chores after supper. Oh, I don't mind. There were times when it wasn't so rushing. Winter times, there was much less time. Though we went out and cut wood, usually, then. I laid up a year's supply of firewood. It was on wooden stoves, of course, and they burned wood, which was the cheapest thing that they had. And most of them had it a little along their places.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Some were sort of lacking in that. And then they spent their leisure time breaking out their... We used to walk through woods on about the last quarter mile in our place. And we used to cut across to what we called the Alder Road. That was on the Alder Place. We caught up a little on the way to school that way, a little shorter way through. And he had a lot of woods down there if we walked through first before we came up to where his fields began. Not a tree on that place right now, except maybe some on a piece of scab ground.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that your mother found the work hard?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, yes. Mother was always a worker. She couldn't stop working. Even when she was old, she was always doubting at something. She was nothing else. She'd make quilts and crochet things and bedspreads and things like that. She was still working on something that went into... She got a guy at the cold, which went into influenza and died. She was 86 then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was well known as a nurse and midwife too, wasn't she?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I don't know when Mother learned that, really. But she knew she'd had children herself. She had midwife children, I think, in Spokane and Moscow when we lived there. Spokane was largely a village of tents when they came there in 1887. And quite a bunch of the Spokane Indians kept nearby. Actually, when Joe was born there, she had an Indian woman help her for a while in the house there. She never bothered too much about Indians, but she thought it was rather too much when the woman's husband had all these kinds of collector wages. She couldn't send the wages home. She didn't let his wife take the wages home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of midwifing, what do you remember being like for doing the midwifing?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I know. They would call on her before we got... Well, let's see. It was about 1998 or 2009 when we got telephones. I was about 8 or 9 years old. The telephones out there, that helped some. But the woman managed to get phones in there. And that made it easier, but they'd come and notify somebody. Sometimes she would go across to American Ridge. And the only way she'd go was either walk or on horseback. It was usually one of the boys that would go with her across the canyons, all the trails. She went over there a couple of times to deliver babies at the Moody House. I don't know if any of the Moody family is still around here or not. There was a Ted and an Ollie.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To deliver the baby, would she actually deliver the baby herself and then would she stay and nurse the mother?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, she'd stay for some little time, so she thought it was all right. Or she could get some help in the house if she needed it, yes. And she delivered some of the air on the ridge. I don't know just how many. She delivered there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was she doing all her own chores as well as that?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, when she was away, on the mid-wifing, we took care of the house. I had an older sister who became 14 the fall of we came to the farm. She was very capable of housewives at that age, I'd say. No way, I think she was. I didn't recall anything about her for several years, you see. I was a baby when they arrived. She always seemed to be grown up at about 13, 14 years older than I was. And there was Edith, she was about three and a half years older than I was, too, later on. But Ellen went out to work early, too. There were so many things that could be done. Now, you don't see prune orchards on Burnt Ridge now, but there were several large prune orchards there, and they had these dryers. They constructed frame-building dryers with racks and things, and knew how to dry their prunes. I wish they lasted until I had to look at them to see how they were operated. But they began taking them down about that time because they found that they didn't make much on prunes, and they did better on wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your sister worked out?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: She worked out a good many times. She went, I think, that same fall. No, not the same fall. That same year that we came out there, she was 14 that fall. When winter came and there was no school out there, she came into town and worked in one of the grocery stores in that winter. They didn't think particularly anything about age, kids going to work as long as they did the work. And I remember she was going off cherry picking and prune picking and apple picking. There were orchards of all kinds that disappeared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would stay at the farmers?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, they'd stay at the farmers. They came into big groups sometimes in these large cherry orchards. Well, they'd bring their own blankets, and they'd find a place for them to sleep in some outbuilding, or sometimes just in some area. They'd laid off these little blankets under the stars. There were a lot of that going on. She worked at Little Bear Ridge, an American ridge. And some of them moved down to start at an early picking down in the Lewiston country, not that far.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She would go by herself?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, there'd be other girls. They generally went in groups. And they did some right on Bird Ridge there for a while. I know the Calibert place out there. Well, they called it the Calibert place. Ernie Anderson lives there now. He was married one of the Calibert daughters. There was a big prune orchard on that one,… and a dryer. And Sanders, we were just opposite us. We had 160, and he had 160 parallel. Still a mile and a quarter mile, and he bought up some old canyon land, farms that people were ready to abandon and sell for any some, because it didn't not pay any more. Past your mind that they were facilities trying to cultivate. Most of them were really that. One fellow, I don't live down there, in the farm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Our home place. Small house. And a sizable family. I don't know how they got them in it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Lived in the canyon?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, in the canyon down there on a little flat, where they could make a garden around it. And then sometime, somebody had occupied before him and had a pear orchard. That's what is now the trout place out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How could he make a living?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, he didn't make a living on that. He worked for the railroad and took his family out there, and they raised a garden at that. He was a section. He didn't make much either. It was quite a family, why they had a spot to live on and raise a garden. If you hadn't spotted one, a little hay for a horse, or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there a number of people living in the canyon at that time?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, there are at least one, two, three, about four houses, old houses, I remember, around the southern part of Burt Ridge, the Point. They were in the woods. Well, it was not very heavy woods on those canyon sides. Well, the other had pretty good timber around it, yes. A little flat. And actually, we color-headed that afterwards, some. We raised hay down there. And fed it to the cattle, usually in the canyon. Cut and stacked it down there. But then around the other side, what they called the Cusie Place. And that was a bigger cabin. But I never did see it when anybody occupied it. They'd gone. And that land was bought by Sanders. He just used it for pastures. I don't know. He must have got it in a cheap, very cheap figure. They wanted anything to get out of it. And up the canyon just right opposite the Rudine Place, you know what that is. Well, Arthur Anderson owned, or Arthur Anderson's son, George Anderson, you know him. He owns it down there with this old brick house. Rudine, Paul Rudine's father.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Paul?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, I didn't. Oh, he died about four years ago.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a widow still, didn't he?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Mike Rudine's out here. You probably met Mike. Yeah, I know he is. Well, his grandfather settled in there, and he bought that land. There was a family named Monahan that lived there. Monahan was a son-in-law of Cusie. And that was a still better house, quite a bit better. And then there was another one. Oh, it wasn't. But a little ways down in the canyon. I don't know how it is. It must have had some top land. He was so close to it. And why he didn't build his house up on top, I don't know. But he didn't. Wheeler was his name. And that was abandoned, too. All those were abandoned. Except this one that was on the place which Sanders bought, our neighbor. First, you picked it up for pasture land. And I remember that family who just rented that place that was living there. It was Homestead by a family in Coveland. But this fella lived there with Hagan. And I don't know how many children there were, but I went down there when I was a little kid, about five years old. It seemed to me an unending stream of little girls around there.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Probably fewer than I thought. You know, in some places I have heard that they would have, some of the neighbors would have disputes over the land and the boundaries and that kind of thing. Oh, that's happened a few times, I think. Actually, that had happened. There was a little fellow on American Ridge. He picked up 80 acres of land, only about 10 acres old. I don't think there were 10 acres. Maybe six or seven on top of the hill. Dad built his house for it, too. And the rest of it was down there. He had an exaggerated idea how much pastries he could get out of that. The steep hillside on American Ridge and timber, not much grass, and reached up a little ways on the Burnt Ridge across that bordering our place. He started surveying it himself. He got his lines all crooked. I think on our side we saw it was covered all together wrong and talked to him about it. His son Fritz came over there and he saw it. He thought it was wrong. He helped straighten his father out so he got that fence. But he'd already started fencing and setting up posts.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: We told Astor Shurair to come out and do it. He was making sure he understood he was wrong. He laid it out down to the corner. And it was way off. 60, 70 yards off of his lines. His lines weren't even straight. He should have known that he was going wrong. I don't know. I don't remember too much about the boundary issues. That probably came before, when they were in the early days, when it was all wooded. They didn't know where to find the stones. They put heavy stones as they found them. They put markings on them that they understood. And sometimes when we did that, we found that they put a stone, sank it right in the roadway before it entered our place. And that was supposed to be a stone there so they started digging down. He took a few measurements. He looked up the road and said it should be the Cali Road, which took so much out to east side, 50 feet wide. He said, this is right in the middle of the road. He says, here it should be here. And he found it, a great big stone that had been buried there as a cornerstone. I don't know. I think that survey had gone through.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But who knew where to find those stones?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: They'd have to have a surveyor out, and most of them couldn't afford that much. So the surveyor had laid stones probably right along. I wonder if there were trees, they probably would have preferred to put a mark on the tree. Well, if there was a tree nearby, the description said so many feet from a tree. That's what it said by this. And they said there was something hung in the tree. I don't know what they said hung in the tree. Anyway, one of the surveyors climbed up and measured out to see if there should be trees. He climbed up the tree and said, here it is. The marking is just about gone since, but this is it all right. It's been here. There's been something that has been wired to it. I think it was a little stone that circled wire around it and put the wire in the trees. The tree had been growing in the meantime, and it embedded the wire into it.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Of course, what they needed was a surveyor's description before they could start looking for those stones. Sometimes those trees got cut down in the process of clearing the land. I don't remember on Burnt Ridge itself of disputes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you hear of any?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Not boundary disputes, no. I was thinking that more general dispute actually. I was thinking about whether you knew of any disagreements among neighbors. There were disagreements among neighbors. Those things took place every once in a while. So I thought the other one was acting like a bad neighbor, not keeping up his share of the fences and things like that. Oh, everything like that happened, I guess. I know one neighbor in the old days, he had a... He had his road, the road he'd sort of made to his house, ran right to a neighbor's place through woods there, came out to the main road. And he'd used it for many years. He sort of regarded it as his own, but actually it wasn't. He was entitled to egress along the line fence and then turn a corner and follow another line fence where they laid the public roads. He didn't like it at all, but nothing came of it.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think he was somewhat ignorant of the law. He thought if he used the road long enough, it became his.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think made it a good neighbor or on the other hand a bad neighbor?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, someone that wasn't looking... if there were things to be done together and he wanted certain work done, he'd help you and you'd help him. And you wouldn't try to be unreasonable in your charges and willing to pay for... Yes, you'd be willing to pay for work he does. Sometimes he's just a change to work, but not to be reasonable about it, to be reasonable about how much work he should have done for the other. And generally keeping up your own shares of the fences that needed to... because... and they had cattle and horses loose in the fields at certain times of the year, like in the fall, before they sold. They often had fields fenced off too, so to think. You think that if... And sometimes, of course, there were just personalities that clash. Scratch to that kind. That was a fairly normal neighborhood, I think, in most places. That group that was there, I don't know. A lot of them were people that were homesteaded. Some of those, quite a few, were original homesteaders. The original homesteaders, there were very few that actually remained too long. The Rubeberg family was one. I think Mrs. Walner owns that part now, and met her down in Burnt Ridge. I don't know if she lives there or not yet. I don't know if that's her. No. And Jack Driscoll, who we bought from it, was one. Not who there... Sanders was one. He was a Canadian morn. So many...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: you would think probably most of that land had changed hands by the time your family came out?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: A large part of it already had changed hands. I don't know who originally was the one where Randy Orr lived there. He was a man who had lived there for many years, but he came a few years after us. He bought it from Girard, but Girard was more of a land speculator than the farmer. He bought some places out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you talk about personality conflict, what would something like that be over?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I have not understood names or anything, but just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would cause it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. It's just... almost anything. The same thing as somebody... had somebody who was a little bit of a bully and tried to run things, and somebody called him on it. I was thinking to be living in the same place for years and years with the same neighbors, that you could have something build up over time. Oh, yes, it's true. They were. Actually, they were seldom lasting these, and very peaceful most of the time. Oh, you did have one hot-headed neighbor there. He eventually moved to, but he lived there a long time. His father was a young man. He was a son that was rather hot-headed. He just got mad about things. But he calmed down as he got older, too. He got married to any family. He was a very quiet friend. But those things happened. I don't know. Or to speculate, I wouldn't really recall what started any of those little things. But I know I heard about these other communities. They'd be fabulous. They'd be huge. They'd start up between a couple of fellows over at disagreement.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did most of the people there go to the same church?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: They did. By the time we got there, the large part of them were. And they got more so. That community became rather sweet in origin. Sanders was not. He was a Canadian. He and his wife. And they had relatives out here. The whole bunch of the Mizzie Sanders had three brothers and his sister. They all came by way from Minnesota. And I think those old folks then moved out their parents first and covered white-in days. They were real old-timers. One of the first settlers probably in the area around Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the church? Was it Church right down to Burnt Ridge?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No. They never had one on Burnt Ridge. They came to Troy. Luther and Church in Troy and still existences. One that had a continuous existence there. I think he was probably the only one that had a really continuous existence with subscribers to it since that time. And there were a lot of Swedes and Norwegians settled north of Troy and eastward toward Derry. And they moved on to that area there, the northern parts of Little Bear and Big Bear Ridges. Well, there was a mixture of course. There there was. Pure roots that had surely one thing or the other. When I started school, well, the Sanders family was living there. But Sanders moved over to Melrose. He got that idea when he went out harvesting, as they called it. They had these long harvests with steam engines. And he was a good steam engineer. And his son usually went with him as the water hauler, the four horse teen. And he worked for somebody over there on the Nez Perce River that wanted him back every year. He could have an engine on Lucid. So he got interested in land over there. And so he sold this place to a fellow who was also trying to speculate the land. Landfare that lived in Troy. An oldish man, a man. But he was getting interested in buying and selling land. He sold it here. Then moved over there. But then he got the bug to go to Canada. And yet some of his brothers were already in the West, had to reach western Canada from eastern Canada. And he spent about three years there. And then he went up and homesteaded in Alberta near Calgary. I talked to his daughter here about five or six years ago. And his youngest daughter, who was a little older than I was, and he died about two years after he got up there. Heart attack. She said the family never knew he had any heart condition. He never told him. Besides, his dad was too old of a pioneer at his age. Because he was a pretty old man to have his younger family. He must have been very late in life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: One thing I was just thinking of is, do you have to know where Burnt Ridge got its name?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I'm not so sure. But they said there had been a fire across that and there was burnt out things. And I believe that there had been a pretty good flame at one time. Because there were still charred trees in our canyon land. But I can remember, if you were standing upright, charred...snags. So it had been some fire up that distance. How much of Burnt Ridge had it covered, I don't know. I'd heard that the Indians did quite a bit of burning. Well, you know, they did burnings. They liked the underbrush to disappear. So they set fire at that time of the year. And let the underbrush get started there. It usually didn't harm the big trees. And then it was kind of clear. Clear enough so that big fires didn't so often start during the summer, during the hot months. Because it really is the small rush at the bottom that gets the big ones started. They have to have enough heat before they start streaking up in the sky and burning the big timber. They used to do that, they said, because they wanted to be able to see through the trees when they were deer hunting in the fall. They would clear it out. They'd do it year after year and it didn't do much damage to the big timber.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And it helped them in hunting. It wasn't easy for the deer to hide. Well, it was logical, they were way hooky there. You know, you're talking about your sister working out and about the boys helping in home-cleared land. And what that makes me really wonder about is what the idea was that the parents held and about whether the work that the kids did was for the kids. Well, I suppose the main thing was they really believed that they should control the kids' incomes. That varied by parents. Some were more liberal than the others in their way of looking at that thing and that the kids usually had some say in how their earnings went. And others didn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your father's idea about that?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, he wanted to make sure that we didn't... Of barley is a little bit... well, here, I got one over here. The recent development of barley is sort of a renewal. There was a time when they almost quit bars. And they got good prices for the oats to make you raise more of it, usually. Much more oats and less barley, please, at one time. Now, those were the only ones. Corn, some people raised some corn crops and some potato crops. But nobody ever made anything great on them. And they were discontinued. Our neighbor that came out from Oklahoma, he lived just north of us. That place is owned by Ed Ramsdale now. But he had tried several things there. And some of that belonging was young and he raised some big corn crops. Raised corn there. Raised corn. Got some big crops on it and he got some potato crops. But usually he didn't have much luck with the sale on those things either. Potatoes would be low and you didn't know potatoes had an uncertain value. And sometimes, oh, well, we had a neighbor. Before we got that place, which once belonged to Sanders, it was rented out for a period of three years to a couple of different farmers. One stayed one year, one stayed two. And the last year, that fell over, they paid just high prices for potatoes the year before. And he thought he'd make it on potatoes. My goodness, the bottom dropped out of it the next year. So much so that he didn't even haul all these potato crops to town. He couldn't make wages on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about corn?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I've kind of wondered about corn. It seemed to be that you could grow in some places. Well, it grows all right. But it can't compete with the Midwest for a corn country. It just doesn't seem to be as good a corn country here. But maybe they took in some of this special hybrid corn they developed in Iowa and Nebraska and those places. And tried to get it to be. But they sometimes often put in more or less as a garden crop, sweet corn. And he was doing a summer, so he had corn on the cob to eat. That's all along. If any gardeners are left, I don't know if there's any gardeners left. Didn't do that. Heck, Bruce did it out there when he was letting Easter deer here. He had little sweet corn. Sweet corn and sunflowers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of species of wheat were people growing on in Berkeley?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, it's changed with the... Well, I mean back around the turn of the century. Well, it was what they called Little Club, along with the club meats. It was succeeded largely by the Jenkins Club, which had a red jazz.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Jenkins Club?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Jenkins Club. The old Little Club and Big Club, as they called them. They named after the... Oh, more about the size of the kernels, or the size of the heads of the kernels. The Little Club usually yielded heavier. The smaller heads, but more of them. They stool out more. And then they began getting... And red Russian was a type. That was a head we had brought in from the Ukraine. And turkey red. And that was a turkey sweet, if it was the name for the country. It was a bearded wheat. Red Russian is smooth. And they were reddish kernels, both of them. A red Russian was a rather soft wheat, but turkey red was a hard wheat. And commanded a little premium. Main trouble was it did not yield as well as the other kind, the soft wheat. And most people acquitted because they got too many less bushels to the acres than could be covered by the premium. Because it was the hardest. Blue stand was an old favorite. It was both a spring wheat and a fall wheat. Turkey red and red Russian were fall wheat. Little Club. Big Club was a spring wheat. Jenkins Club could be planted either way. And then I remember several of the other... We began getting hybrids. Hybrid number 128. Hybrid number 143. We raised both of those kinds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you do a lot of experimenting? People in general in those days?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, they would try to do wheat as they came out. And new varieties of oats. I remember what they called side oats. Instead of just a spray of heads, they were encusters. Four or five, six, a side. Hanging to the sides of oats. Looked kind of unbalanced. So we'll talk. I don't always see if it was as good as any other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever hear about Adams Alaska wheat?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: That fellow from Juliaetta who was putting out... Well, there was what they called the Alaska wheat. I don't remember it too well. But it was introduced down here. Maybe it's the same or not. I know it's Adams. This fellow in Juliaetta was pushing his own wheat that he called the Alaskan wheat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, lately?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, this was around 1910. Yeah. Maybe that was it. I didn't recall much about that. Except there was a so-called Alaska wheat. But I sort of imagined that there was one, those hardy wheat that they could grow around Fairbanks on a short season basis in the summer.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I was going to ask you, could you describe to me pretty much what the season was like, what you would do for the planning and take care of and then harvesting the crop, and when you started it and when you finished it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, starting the seeding, you tried to get as much fall wheat as you could. And then entailed, before they started raising beans, entailed the summer fall to seed it long in September or, I mean, October or November, usually. They wanted to get good rains on it and sprout it up. And there was some difference there. I remember it would be called spent wheat that was much raised and lasted longer and probably still used 40-fold wheat. Because you may have a proper time to put it in. Most people felt it was the last thing before the snow fell. Just to have enough moisture to sprout it underground, it lay there until spring and come out in the spring. So it was tricky trying to determine just how late they should risk it, of course. And they tried a normal way when they started raising beans, was to use the bean ground and get that in as soon as they could after the beans were harvested off. The bean harvest was usually later than the wheat harvest. Yet the wheat harvest done mostly in August and the bean harvest might last into September. And then you'd sow that and then you'd go to plowing up the things and try to get all the plowing done possible in the fall. The idea was to get it all done, but the type of season you had sometimes interfered.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And then on the land, rains couldn't determine how much rainier or how dry a fall you had. We had falls where there was mild, not even if you had a little rain to plow to the end of November. That's where they stopped by the first November.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there falls where you couldn't get the wheat in at all?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I don't remember any during our own, on our own farm at that time when I was a kid. But they always managed to get the farm again sometime. But I think there have been, on the two falls like that, I've heard of, that it started raining in September and didn't quit long enough to get the fields dry enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We were the only crop that you'd put in in the fall, weren't you?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yeah, there was a fall barn in the fall there was. But I don't believe too many of them used fall barley. They were too afraid they might freeze out. They wouldn't understand the winter, so they put it again in the spring. Olds is strictly a fall crop and much more popular the way the years were like without there than it is later on. Olds was planted in spring. And barley, they planted barley.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But people didn't want to plant their wheat in the spring, did they?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, if they couldn't get it in the fall like that, then they would plant it in the spring. And some split, but in spring wheat too. Sometimes the fall, the winters were too severe and the fall wheat was slow coming out of it in the spring. Cold springs, it wasn't any better than if they were to plant it in the spring. They were what they called spring wheats and fall wheats. They had blue stem and big club in the old days with the principle of spring wheats. Later on Jenkins Club was used in the spring too. And then they had the fall wheats.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of winter did DeFarmer hope for for his wheat?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, what you hoped for was not too cold winter. And since they knew they were going to be freezing or more, sometimes or other, they liked a good snow cover. Because the top of the snow would absorb the freezing. And it isn't like Alaska where it freezes the soil a long ways down. It would be kind of frost free on the surface if they had a good covering of two feet of snow. That would be a nice amount to have over the fall wheat until it melted in the spring. And then if they could avoid a hard freeze after the snow had gone, they were sort of set for that. That was one of the risks of course of fall wheat. It would snow and disappear under Chinook winds or under rain. And it would have a hard freeze on that stuff before you got a little snow cover. That is what is called winter kill is the term. It was generally used. It was still used I think for a fall wheat planet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I guess for actually all of this around here now is fall wheat isn't it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yeah, the wheat. I don't think too many people would find spring wheat except to say they are stuck. Right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, were the worship of winds pretty common then?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, yes. We always thought of getting one or two during the winters. And then you hoped for snow before it got real cold. Yes. Get another little cover of snow before a freeze had come. So sometimes you would lose part of that. Oh yes. It would be damaged. I don't remember that we ever had a dot on our farm, but one so damaged that we replanted. It looked fair enough to bring a good crop. And I think our fall wheat was the best thing we ever had there really. It was a steady diet. It really made a big difference then to get it in the fall rather than in the spring. Beans were very good for a while and the price was so comparable enough that you didn't need to have some. Later on they raised beans with a lot more pounds to the acres, but then the expenses grew on them. And they priced and rise in proportion, taking all the harvesting the beans take. They take cutting. Don't handle them with a combined harvester or you'll shatter them out. They'd be cut together and put through a fishing rig, stacked, let dry a little, let dry a little and then haul them in or stack them. One to two. They often stacked them because they were felt the weather would be too undefendable at that time of year in September or late August to risk your age all along them. Because then if they would start laying there drying out, these seeds would start popping. The pods would start popping open.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They would stack them and then dry them out?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, they let them dry. Then they'd stack and top them generally with a canvas or with straw and wait for a thresh machine to get around. That was one of the ways. Of course the first thresh machines that I know, all threshed out of stacks. They stacked their grain. There weren't so many thresh machines that they could get to. You didn't know how many weeks you'd have to wait before you were getting threshed. You had to wait an awful long time one year before you got the old rig on the, they had an old horse power rig. By the way, I saw we were going through my sister-in-law's pictures here. They brought them out here. There was a picture of her father, Mr. Cummings. He had marked an arrow and that was he there. They didn't see where they got the power. Somebody asked me. Oh, it was Darcelle's husband. Maybe it was the Sunday law. Well, here it was. A group of about six teams going round and round on a power machine. That was hooked through the cable. If not a cable, it was a rod really. They turned over and over and turned the thresh machine. That's the first kind I remember. They had that kind on Burnt Ridge the first time I saw them. That year, that thing couldn't work. It went out of commission and left the Pearson family. We lived there where Slim Smith lived. Our family and Porter, he had the thresh machine. We lived down there below Slim Smith. I don't know if there was a house at the edge of the canyon. I don't know if there was a house left there or not. That was mostly a canyon. One was very small on the up. He owned the thresh machine. He was the thresh machine man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He had left these families without ration?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, he had very little, yes. He had his family living there, but he did very little farming. He was a horseman. He did horse work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What I mean is after, this went out of commission?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, this other one here, it was just gone. It didn't know how to fix it or too expensive. It was owned by a few people on stock industry that bought it together. So he came down at the end of the season. He'd been out threshing quite a ways away from Troy, his thresh machine. When he pulled in, he refreshed our, a Pearson's first up there, which is nearest his place. Then he came down and threshed ours. The first steam rigs were on burnt ridge. He flashed those two places. At the end of the season, we had ours grain stacked, of course, like they did in those days. We'd had rain in between. It was dried off at that time. So, all right, I remember they all left that thresh machine over that grade coming up the road there, from our place in there, threshed. Sanders, Sanders wasn't raising any grain those days, hardly. I think maybe he had moved away that year, as possible, he had.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that why they stacked it instead of leaving it there?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Because... Because they wanted to protect it against the rain. Protect it against rain, because they didn't know how long it would be before they could get threshed. Then they went over towards leaving it in the field when they felt more confident that the threshing machines would make it. Because I know on later years they did a lot more of that bundling. Oh, yes, they started that. When you had a season of 25 days, threshing season could be done in about 25 or 30 days. They went to leaving it in the bundling. Sometimes they had to wait two months for thresh machines.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what changed it? Just the better machine, right?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, they got more machines in. More people could buy machines. There were an awful lot of people that didn't have much cash to buy anything. And there were very few thresh machines. Old-fashioned types, too, and they got better thresh machines. It worked. Not many years after that time there, oh, I think, let me see. I know we had thresh machines came in on Park Ridge from outside for a couple of years. Pain. There's still pain families around Troy. He'd be a grandfather or a great-grandfather to most of them. He brought one in there once. And... Hokanson. Hokanson and Gunderson. There's still Hokansons, too, I know, around here. From Bay Ridge, they brought a thresh machine in. And then the farmers went together and purchased one. They got the price of a new machine,… and everybody they wanted to put in as many dollars as they thought he could, so they got enough to buy it. The thresh and wavy engine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was joint owned?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: It was a joint owned, with each man having so much stock yet, according to how much he put in. We were in on that, and nearly all the farmers were. Oh, I believe a few of the very small farmers didn't go into it, and some that were renters didn't go into it. Excuse me a minute. I think... Well, now, if you had good weather in the fall, and you were planting on bean ground,… on which cultivators, of which weed cultivators, had been run during the summer, and are still pretty loose and weed free, the thing to do, probably the best thing to put on it, was a spring tooth harrow. Now, this goes over in springs, and pulls in high. And that usually was very good at leveling little spots in the air, and usually loosened it up enough you could see right in it. Sometimes if there was a little too much weed handled in there, it's probably better to use a spring tooth harrow on top of the spring tooth, or afterwards, smooth it out after seeding. Sometimes you just put it in and you're through. That's when you have a spring tooth and you see it had no drill. That's if you had bean ground. Of course, on summer fall, you usually use this disc harrow. You roll the disc using them too, and clean that up. And then you usually had to use a spike tooth on that to level it, to get out those ridges that the disc formed. And then put the drill in. Usually you didn't need to put a harrow afterwards, sometimes people did, to make it smoother as food as possible for winter. And figured once the snow was on, it would pack evenly, and they wouldn't be much washing. Usually if they had always on these drills a little change back in the shoes at which the seed went into the ground, it was very good at leveling. If you had one with those good little drags on them, they were made different. They were summed up much better than others. It acted much like a leveler.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Now for beans, the spring's dead on, it is best to start with the cultivated with the prolly. It has been let lie open during the summer to absorb as much moisture as possible for the, for the, for under, under the ground moisture. And generally leveled it. Sometimes had to level it, it was real bumpy and been washed by heavy, with several thaws and several that, with a spike tooth harrow. That's the level it was. And then usually it's better to just, sometimes you put on discs on, not so bad. Sometimes you put the spike, you might put the spike tooth first and then the disc. Other times you'd reverse that, according to the condition of the ground, to get it level. And then it'd be, it'd be as early as possible, but it didn't need to be done too early. There was no use preparing it too early. Because before, we always considered it dangerous to plant much before mid-May, because of late freezes. Because you get the crop in, it has the ground cold, the freeze and the ground cold, and beans, wooden sprouts are good. But sometimes earlier it's 10th of May, so unlike that, and plant all the way into mid-June. Could be, could be planted as late as that. And sometimes the weather forced postponement. So that's when the water starts early as they could, and if they felt the ground had warmed up sufficiently, it would sprout beans well. That, you nearly always had that harrowed. And often they used to run a roller on it. Some people had these log rollers, they're homemade. The big log, they bore the hole right through the center and put a rod through there, and this log was used to roll ground, make it even, break down all the clods and have it real smooth. Some used what they called a clod measure. Those were usually homemade too. Made of heavy planks, sort of built one slanting this way, one slanting under it a little ways. So they'd fit down, and if you had a little rough field, and it dried out enough there were some pretty big clods in it. Yet, you could waste it if you wanted to. And you won't just use stones or anything, you could waste it to make it sufficiently heavy to smash those things down. They were generally a two horse rake, horse sticks, and the roller was a two horse rake too. One, two of them, one of them is here ahead of me, one is with me, and got rented a room together in the place there, and did their own cooking in the little upstairs part of the house. They were from outside.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: From outside the Troy area?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, not outside the Troy area. Both of them lived up here on the Bosco Road somewhere. And both of those went on to school afterwards, college, and so on, and they were intended to get there. And there's an awfully scant amount of kids. Well, there were quite a bunch of those that went to that school that finished the high school that went on further. If they went that far, they seem to go on. Yeah. A lot of them. So there were a number of kids from Troy that... Yes, I...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As much as a majority of the graduating class from high school?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, now, with my graduating class, I think they all did. I think there were only seven in my graduating class, down here. They started out a good deal more than what they dropped out along the line. Moved away, some of them. I don't know why. Some of those might have gone on the school and moved away, and some just didn't. One or two died rather young. One died, oh, in second year. He died one summer after a few years of school was out. But there, I think every one of those went to school somewhat.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, why? What was the thinking of people like yourself? Why? Why go to college?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I think about that time they were understood more or less why you were going. By that time, there was less every year about this. They saw people, others, going away. There were several that had gone away from birth rates to go to school, for instance, before I did. You see, Joe was probably the first one I think that ever did, but he was nine years older than I was. In the meantime, I don't know, several of the Pearson children went away to school. Well, professionally, one of the girls became a nurse. And one of the boys became a dentist. One day, we went on to school and just took about a year, went to work at the bank. But progress right along when he once got there. He was the cashier in the branch of the Bank of America. In Los Angeles, we were in the Los Angeles area at least, when he was hired.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But to go to college, were people going there because they expected a career out of going? Was that the main reason?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think the most of them. That was true. They all expected a career out of it, practically. I said it was mostly what people, the people I knew at the university were there for. They expected a career out of it. But I went to school because an awful lot of students at the school there were partly paying their own way by working. Well, some during school years and some would lay out a couple of years and then come back. I don't know, Mick. I think the boys in college were almost universally pointing for a career of some kind. Some of the girls may not have been. Never explained themselves to me. I think more of them just were going to college along with their friends. Or because their parents wanted them to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they were looking at making money in the short term before they were married and that sort of thing?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, maybe some of them did. Actually, there were some of those there. There were ones like my sister who got a primary certificate for teaching. The first went out and taught and later returned. There were women there in schools who had three, four, and five years of teaching behind them coming back to finish out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you decide you didn't want to be a farmer? What? Why did you decide you didn't want to follow in your father's footsteps and be a farmer?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, the thing is, to get farms, apply money. You don't divide up those kinds of farms between children because they wouldn't be, you just create a hardship on them. The places that were meant for one family were more than one. I knew very well of the waltz and pauls of budget farmers as I'd ever been. I was always interested to study. I ran a school that said go that way. Especially after Joe and Carl went into school. You kept harping it, that's the other one, you said get an education.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the prospects to stay in the area? Was that important to most of the young people? Did they want to stay here?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I guess there was something about that. Yes, they would like to stay in the same area. A lot of them did. Maybe some of it was just because they had certain things to keep here. No, this didn't. A lot of them moved out. It usually depended on the size of the family. Five boys in our family, five boys in the Kelbert family, five boys in the Newbury family, four in the Pearson family, four in Frank Swanson's family. All the well-trained, well the great range would be about 15 years of all those people's age range. They couldn't all stay on the farm, so some of them knew they had to do something else. And they got left, all of those kind of moved out. Herman Rupert remained on the Rupert place out there. He was the oldest boy and his mother was widowed by that time. And he married rather late in life.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: All the Pearson boys went. Willie and Walt were undoubtedly stayed where they lost health, eventually died. Adolf farmed for a while and he said he didn't like it. He was too uncertain of life. He might have a good crop, he might have a bad, he wanted something more steady, she said. And Joe never tried it. I don't think he would have cared for it either. He decided at that time the farm didn't have any attractions for him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did one of the boys take the farm?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: None of them did. They eventually sold it out to the father of Rudolph Smith, who lives there now. So there there was a farm that nobody wanted in the kids. One of the best farms none of the kids wanted to know. This is Pearson who moved to Portland where one of her daughters was married. And Edla was down there training to be a nurse, one of the girls. She had a couple of younger children with her. A youngest daughter I guess too. She was not grown up really. Well she grew up down there and married. And then by that time Joe had started working in the bank. Adolf was probably about completing dentistry. It went on. There was family, no they just cleared out. And it's one of the best farms on the range. I don't know if I had a selection of the farm on the ridge. I'm not so sure. I'd take that. Except for one thing, they had had problems with water. Ed from the earth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, for yourself, could you have stayed and taught in this county, in this area if you'd wanted to?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. I just took one of the first ones I had an opportunity to take. That was in State of Washington. Found it available and I accepted it. I don't know why I just got it. No, I didn't really want to. To stay here. To stay here. I wanted to try some other place. I'd moved around quite a bit in the early days. I wasn't too satisfied with teaching at all. I was left teaching when I was about 35.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why weren't you satisfied with it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, very serious. The pave was never too good. Up to the time I elected. And it's an entirely worrisome job, I found. Trying to... Teach people interested in their work. I can see why... ...Osher's is one of the common complaints of teachers. I think it's because the kids that you're teaching are interested in the work. The lack of interest. All these are sort of an undercurrent of a little noise. Things happening. I want you to see that they all get a test to... ...to learn their sightings. I taught mathematics a good deal. The only thing that was definite, you knew what the kids were getting and what they weren't getting, but it was somewhat different when it came to actually teaching a student that had no mathematical sense at all. He was completely frustrated. There were two ways of looking at that.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: He was kind of at the shoulder side of the little dirt and mud to get into where it was. That is, every winter I suppose there was a certain amount of soil swept down that hillside. But it sort of covered it over.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Ice all year round, right?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I wonder what it was like, what it looks like on the inside. Well, in crystals, he said, hanging in ice. There had been apparently a lot of water that had frozen at one time. It stayed that cold inside. It stayed that cold inside, just what caused it, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I sort of wondered about why an ice mine, why doesn't it melt?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: But, you know, we're talking about leaving the area. I'm really interested in that because you see most of the old timers I talked to are the people that stayed, never left. Yes, I suppose everyone's a huge side here. The ones I find, right. But that's only part of the story, of course.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what do you think about yourself?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: You didn't really have any particular desire to stay.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why do you think that was?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. I just, I don't know, starting in on something in your own home, on your own home people, I think it would be the hardest job of all. People have known you all your life. I went to another town, I was accepted as a teacher. So I tried teaching at Troy and said, here's Bud Carlson. I don't think any, some people have gone back to their own home towns and liked it. Established themselves. And, apparently, that's what Roger Swanson, the superintendent, has done. But for most people, I think it's better to be away from their own home town, the home home people. They can make it among strangers a lot easier. They might come to a family which had never been fond of another family. And those things sometimes last through generations. They're not old time Kentucky feuds, but those things persist. That would make it unpleasant for, in your work, or just in... It could. It would make you unhappy, personally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about friendships, though? Can you develop more friendships that are as deep as the ones that you left behind?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think so. You should stay alone long enough.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think you might have gone into farming if you could?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Could have. Oh, I don't know if that would or not. Maybe I could have, but then I knew it was... But I didn't really have the desire to… i don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What does he do?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: You see the trying parts of it, as well as the good parts when you grow up on a farm. Sometimes a lot of work you put in for nothing is sought over again, you see. The poor crop yields, the poor price years, and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You agree with that Pearson boy that said that it's too risky?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes. He had something there. He said he didn't think he'd care to farm. He tried it one year. He'd been in high school and he'd studied agriculture. Some agriculture courses there, and he got really interested in how it ought to be done. When he faced a practical problem, getting into crops, getting harvested, and his delays, and getting work done due to weather and all that, in the end he'd be concerned that the practical everyday life of a farmer is in the field.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about most of the younger generation from Burnt Ridge? And most of them were in the area?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, I would say so. There were five of the six rubric children left. Herman eventually left too, but he left when he was quite a long years. He and his wife sold out. He went to Pullman and he worked a little while longer around. He worked for the state college for a while, until he retired. But, yeah, this all went out to work. Clarence became a barber. Victor became an automobile mechanic. The two of the youngest, Ben Shugo and Richard, went to college. Now, Shugo went to work for the, I guess, an electric company in California. He spent many, many years at it until he retired. Richard worked for that same company for a while. He experimented with other things. He ran a taxicab service for a few years. Then he went back for a refresher course in education and finished as a teacher. The girl, she worked out in Spokane for quite a while. She was a housekeeper for a family and then she married up there. She married again after her first husband died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think girls left the area as much as boys did?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, a lot of them left, yes. Some married men lived here. And a few lived on here. A lot of them left. Some had something to do with property, anything like that. They stayed for other reasons, I guess. Some stayed with our folks a long time when they were old, as they were getting older. But there were quite a few of the families that moved out. Kelberg's daughters, all except Helen, who married Ernie Anderson, who brought out the other heirs, who was going to settle up there. He was the only one that stayed here. Well, Elvaro married a man who became a missionary. One of them married a man. She lived in Moscow, it still does. She went on to school too, both before and after she married. She didn't work at the university there, I think she was a lab assistant. Something I ain't not sure how she was. One of the boys tried farming, two of them tried farming at home, fine not there. One of them went on to get to Reddy, to have Reddy land elsewhere, and one quit to become a mechanic.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: One of them married a girl and continued to live right there on the farm. She held land. One boy went back east, he wanted to become a minister, but he found a grind for the education, a little tough. He left there, I don't know what he did, he worked as a truck driver part of the time. He had a shop at one time, one of his brothers told me, to make a pair of shoes, two of them had a pair of shoes, and he scattered out. One of them tried farming too for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The Kelbergs?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, and the two left and went up and worked in Spokane. That was a kind of work. It sounds like getting a college education was somewhat unusual though, for the younger generation on. Well, yes it was, I guess for the ones my age, and below my age it increased. For my age and older it was rather rare. So it was a trend towards getting a college education. A trend. The two youngest Reubard boys were younger than I, the rest were older. The two younger went to college. None of the oldest Kelberg children went, but, well, Hilma started it, actually, and she started it late after she became nearly a young woman. After drop down, well, Teddy went for a while, but he left it. He left the Fayetteville High School, and he did go for a while, and then dropped out and tried fresh farming, and then he got a job in Spokane, which suited him better, because he stayed there from World War II onwards. About that time.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Lately Russell, well, there was Elba. She's another one, she had Hilma. The other ones, that family, that really persisted in school.
