And Alder sent his children there, that, well, the older girl was older, but she was one handicapped, and her youth, by having a feeling she had to stay home after her mother died, with all the younger children around. She probably started before I did. But the younger ones went on to school a good deal, and Gladys, who didn't marry, made a profession of it. Well, I'm sticking to the families there. One of Paul Radine's boys went on to college, the other didn't. It was more a question of choice, I think. And the children of those fellows who stayed here and married, they all seemed to be going on to school, a lot of them. And, well, some of Ed Swanson's girls went on to school, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: College?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: College, yes. I don't know how many were college graduates. Well, Rowena went into nursing, and it's different. But I think one of them married too young to finish college, or even maybe got in there. But there were others who I guess I think are wrong. Well, if you try to put on your statisticians hat and think about it in a very rough way, how would you size up the... Probably the trend has been upward all the time. People going through high school, which is what we're all trying to do now, and going on to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of your generation, would you guess one out of four would go to college, maybe?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I doubt it. No, I don't think so. Probably maybe one out of six. Yes, I don't know. But the percentage of the populations in school, of the certain age groups, was pretty slow about the time I started college. I suppose maybe you could find statistics, but it was rather low. I'm thinking in terms of the area, in terms of Burton, Washington. And even the Troy area. Well, I don't know. It's just different too much from the general trend. I suppose they sent a certain number of you to school too. I know. In the classes I went to in high school, several of those people went on to school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what about leaving the area? How about that? Do you think there was a large percentage of the younger generation left and left this county?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think there must be quite a few years. There are some that have been there a long time, but there are, depends on what you mean, back 40, 50 years. There's a lot of them, and never people that have come in since that time. But if your age group of the younger, the kids of the farms on Burnt Ridge, probably a pretty solid majority of them left and didn't stay here. Oh, yes. I'd say a solid majority left. If you were going to say now... The Smiths family stayed. Two of them are still around, and one was here a large part of his life too. One moved down to Oregon. I think he was just farming down there. And the girls both married to none of their farm right in the city.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: If you were going to guess, and I know this is just guessing again, would you guess maybe four out of five would leave for the younger generation? Four out of five?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. I think maybe three out of four. Or one generation dovetails into another, it would be a little hard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you think the trend towards staying or leaving changed through time? Or do you think it's always been a high out migration?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think it's been an out migration regularly. It used to probably not so much if there's a... Well, the families are smaller nowadays than they were then. My other family's a birch ridge, eight, five, twelve, six, nine, five, six, five, it's nearly everybody, four, it'd be about the smallest I can remember except for one or two. It's just three and one. But most of them are those high numbers, which we call high numbers now. I've got another thorny question along those lines. You're thinking about the impact of the car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the car made it easier for people to stay in the area because it could go farther to work or think it made it more likely they'd leave the area because they could get away easier?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. That doesn't affect. The cars, I think it's a little easier on them. They like the life a little better. They can get places quicker. I don't know. I think it tended to make life more livable for the farmers. Get out a little once in a while easier. I think they helped to keep people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People still wondered?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, yes. When I was a small kid, it was not too many years before that. It was before we moved there, but Lawrence Johnson lived there, Clarence's dad. He was one of the men that took him off. But nobody knew him, but that's the funniest thing I ever heard of, too, it seemed to be. If that description I heard of it was right, I would have thought they had taken him to Troy and put him aside somewhere and see if somebody could recognize him. But they didn't seem to, nobody that was looked at knew him. Those people that found him, I think it was the kid that found him down there. And that just, they don't even know he might have happened himself as far as they knew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So they had no idea what might have happened or anything?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, there was no real clue that they had. Of course, I don't know what to investigate. I don't know if an officer came down to investigate or not. I don't think they didn't. They just jumped the door, they told him what happened. And then nobody seemed to know who he was, so they buried him. And a rather free and easy way before he got down.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they bury the guy?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know that. Maybe they buried him with a mercury sander. Well, I was wondering. I did Clarence Johnson to tell you that. Yeah, I should ask him. I really had to ask him. Because I think his dad was one of those who went down and carried the man up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they find him? Was it Camp Canyon?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, yes, down that way. I don't exactly know where it was either. I think it was between Camp Canyon, like in Burnt Ridge and Barridge. And I think it was down along that canyon side there. Just how far down I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any speculation about where he was down there?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: There was speculation. Everybody speculated. Nobody could think of who might hang his...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because they didn't know who he was, why would anybody want to hang him?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Without knowing who he was, they wouldn't even have clues to who is... ...Eneries by a beach. I only think I could think of maybe good salandis or something like that. Yes, well, as far as anybody knew, there wasn't any such thing. There was nothing like that. Do you ever hear of the rumor that I had heard about that there was a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...called a wild man in Burnt Ridge? There was a wild man in the canyon or something like that? A wild man?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I wouldn't know. I never heard of that. The only one that I heard spoken out was what the fella called Wild Dave.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, did you know him?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yeah. He lived toward Moscow Mountain. He used to come into Moscow more than Troy. But I guess he appeared there too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he supposed to have been by?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Dressed roughly and had a big shaggy beard and wore guns. That's about all. I think he'd come in and get drunk and talk big. As far as I know, he never heard of anybody. But he looked as if he hadn't been cleaned up in five years, I guess, or something like that. People pinned that tag on him. I think the name was Coventry he went to, Dave Coventry. That's right. You know, a book came out and it was signed William F. Grannon. And they told us it was life in the West. And they looked at the picture and that and many of them swore that picture was a wild ape. Our neighbor who lived out there one year renting the Sanders place, his name was Sturdivant. And they had that book. I can outside pick my panties. They had the book and... They had a book and the picture looks so much like Wild Dave. They swore that he must be the guy who had that book put out. He called himself William Grannon in that picture.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he was the same person?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I don't know. I never saw Wild Dave myself. I've heard of that even down to today. People think they were the same person. I myself don't. And the picture in that book. I think that this Wild Dave wrote that book, but I don't see why he would have changed his name. No, I don't either. He impressed everything with a literary person. As that was written, I read the book. Oh, he told the rise of the adventures. He's in relations with Kit Carson and Indian wars. And he had one thing on there, a picture of fellow supposedly, well, I guess maybe he was drawn by an artist who was a fellow. He called it the American Cannibal. That he killed an 80's squall on winter in the mountains. And there was a hard winter and he couldn't get out. I don't know why. He was a little sensational that way. Most people reading the book thought he wrote a bunch of lies, he was telling by himself. Whoever wrote the book. Yeah, I think since then it's been documented that he never did most of that stuff. It was a bunch of lies, a good story. He never did kill Kit Carson. You know, you...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I've never seen another copy of that book, have you seen it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I haven't seen that book, but I know it's out. Probably out of publication. Probably. Well, I never knew there was a book that the family moved in and they had that book and it loaned it to us. And we all read it around there. Well, you know, your brother's role, active in the Democratic Party. I was wondering when your family got...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In the politics?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yeah, I needed to be in Democrats. Oh, I'll tell you, Paul was really the first one that acted in it. Most of us have not been active in politics at all. My brother Carl and Joe and Walter, they were usually voting Republican ticket. I always vote for Democratic ticket mostly. I thought... I'd heard that your father had socialistic ideas. Yes, he did. He had very strong socialistic ideas in the old Socialist Party. But we were in this country. Which then such as Benson and Hillquit. And those were active. But they used to pull... There was a good deal of bolts pulled by the Socialist Party in different ones back about 1908. Or... They pulled about half a million bolts. And there were several times after that they pulled up to a million bolts or so. But then it kind of died out in the 20s, disappeared. I think 1920 itself was the first... last year it pulled a sizable number of bolts.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that with Debs?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: That was with Debs. And Benson was running after that, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where do you think your father got his convictions from?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Old reading on various things. He read a great deal on politics and social and economic theories. He was a reader all his life, I guess, but I know. He'd collected quite a library of various books. Largely on religion and history.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think his views on socialism came from Sweden?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, I think it was after he came over here. Well, he... There were some... You know, the populist party wasn't exactly socialist. It was in... Good years there in the late 1800s. But it was very much more socialistic than the democratic party has ever been. Yes, and such follows Ignatius Donnellan, who ran for... was the candidate one year for their presidency. But I think died before he... They had to... during the campaign they had to change. And he'd been Lieutenant Governor of Minnesota. He was active in labor organizations in the populist party. We had a book by him at home, but it wasn't... That wasn't... That was a prediction novel set in the future. Something like Edward Bellamy's Looking Backwards. Only he... He was not encouraging like Bellamy. He thought that things were going to be better. He thought things were going to be worse. He was a pessimist.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Seems like maybe he was right. Maybe he had something too. These are probably probably completely right. Your father holding views like that, he was probably one of the few people around here who was... Well... Right around this area that was that strong. Conjections, yes. Well, he was one of the few. There were some... There were... There were not very great people. There was hardly nobody else, I don't think. But Don Ron Troy, this fellow is... Oh, I forget his name now. Jeanette, who was active at that party. Socialist party. Socialist party. And... There were one or two others that... Were in it when they established that socialist newspaper in Lewiston that ran for several years before it folded.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Remember the name of that one?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: The Inland Echo. And later they called it the Inland Empire, which changed the name when they... I don't know, they got it passed from one hand to another because they were hard. Then it folded up. Was your father...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father get to know some of the other people that were involved in it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, there was Underwood. It was the name of one, and I can't think of the name of one fellow that was... The socialists had a picnic in Troy one time. I took over the Fourth of July celebration. And organized it. And they had speakers up here that talked a good deal. And they had some speakers up there. I know I was 11 or so with the... I heard a little of it, but I was not too interested in hearing him talk. I was with a bunch of kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your father helping with that?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, I don't think he was. I think... The fact is, I think that was the same year that he went to Sweden about 1912. He took a trip back to Sweden to see his people back there about that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was Underwood from? Is he...?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, Underwood, I don't know where he was from originally.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he in Moscow or...?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, he was in Lewiston this time, but he was from somewhere else. And he was a literary man of some kind. He had published a book of poetry, I think, and things like that. I just sort of never heard a thing about him afterwards.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As far as your father's convictions go, to be a socialist again, what do you think that that meant? The kind of way you'd like to see society run?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, he thought that it was what they might call more pure socialism. The public control of things publicly used, like resources, mining, and food vats, and mines, and things that had to be used by the public, and limit private property to an individual needed for himself. A house, because of something that would be private property. That's for his own use. But land needed to produce food. That's a public utility. That's for public use. That was nearly... That was just about the creed of socialists of that period. They changed both before and after, I'm sure. I know. But that was their... The kernel of their beliefs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there public control of things used by the public?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: The hills, and mines, and water powers, and land, all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he envisioned that people would have more or less the same amount of money to live on?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I don't think no. He knew that that was true. Some people needed more wages because there was more demand for them. The technician needed to have... It ought to be employed at a higher salary than the rent. No technician was tall and it took no education for his work. Not that way. But he was pretty sympathetic towards the working man. Oh, yes. He was very sympathetic. He thought they got the worst part of the deal. The working man got the worst part of the deal in our setup. I think he was very sympathetic. I think they still were getting it in any way. You know, what's interesting about it, to me, in part, is that generally, the farmers... I don't think they've been too sympathetic towards labor, you know, the labor force. Well, no. That's right. So your father... The farmer had a kind of an independence, he felt, that if he could make it on this land he had. And he found that he was doing it year after year, maybe laying a little society, because they did lay a little society and got more things for their homes and all that sort of thing. They improved it. They built new barns and new houses and all that.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I don't think any of the houses on Burke Ridge were the ones originally put up. So your father was kind of huge different in that way. He was looking, it seems to me, a little more farsighted about the way everybody was in the same boat. Yes… he..… we had a schoolteacher out there that wants to talk a great deal of things with us. He was a pretty good teacher. We often wondered from the subject if he was talking on things and had us... He encouraged kids to read the newspapers and that, know what was going on now. He talked about it to him every once in a while. He was one that, if he found, was teaching some subjects or something else, came up related and he used illustration. He went on to talk about that. He'd have went to class time for one particular class or anything like that. He held the interest of students and just keep right on talking about it. And I don't know, I think the club is good at teaching his age. I think he's learned much in the long run. He talked about current affairs. And he talked about current affairs. And he said about these things, he says, they're... those things don't always happen at once, he says. Now the Socialist Party has lots of things in it. The other parties are going to take over. A lot of those things that they advocate, he says, well, probably we'll be in use someday, but they won't be called socialists and they do. Well, that's happened, I don't know. They were the first party to back women's suffrage, the socialists were. They were the first ones that advocated the graduated income tax. And quite a few other things that have come to pass. One time or another. We don't ever think of socialism as just things that they advocated in their platform for that particular time. They had, besides their colonel, they took sides on issues that grew up before the country at that time. The party, they always did that, they had the incorporate, those things exactly in their ideology, that's the things that were done.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your father ever get a chance to be active at all?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, actually, he sympathized a little bit with that, the Inland Echo, that they were going to put on the socialist papers. They may have put a few dollars in, and I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he try to talk to his neighbors?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, well, oh, if political discussions came up, they opened it up, but otherwise he didn't. He'd give his side of these things, and then he'd talk fluidly on that. He was never at the loss for words. If an argument came up, he'd just pursue it right on. And he could get rather heated, too. But then all our neighbors around there, they knew his beliefs and he knew theirs. They talked about something else, about the farm crops or how the other family was or something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the IWW, what did he think about them?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I don't remember him saying anything about it, but well, that came along the arrow. About 17. 17, and he didn't have much use for those things. Those kinds of organizations didn't help anything, as far as I remember. Of course, I don't know what the IWW attempted organizing industrial workers of all kinds together. But some of their organization then were trying to give it a bad day. Probably half of those people that they thought were IWW weren't. I know some people saw us. Coming through the country looking for words. They might be IWWs. Some of the people were too scared of IWWs. They had to see what out of every bush, like some of the people saw communists in everything about the time that the First World War was over. There were some investigations here and there. Well, I know that there was some people were saying that the fires in the fields, in the harvest fields, were being set by IWWs. There was some fires sometimes. I don't know whether they were set or not. I don't think they ever came to truth. So now smut in the wheat. That's kind of a disease. You sometimes get that in the wheat and that stuff could explode if you were running a machine hard. And smokers of all kinds were around these fresh machines too. They used to follow the, they worked in these and a lot of them had a permanent crew. And they just bent down in the haystack at the end of the day. And they were blankets. And it was just as likely a lot of those things were made from careless sources as from anybody else. These yields.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: When you got out of the age, you were a grade on the production of the field. Well, I was out one year. I finished high school in Troy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you went to high school?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Then I went to university in Idaho state that year. University up here later in California. But I laid out a year in between there. One year I wasn't in school. But I went to, that's all I missed, I went to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go in service at all?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, I was in for one that didn't even interrupt my college education. They put what they called the Student's Army Training Corps on the campuses during the First World War. That wasn't the ROTCs that they had in these, but a regularly enlisted group. And they started calling them up as they came to them. I didn't know they were calling the older ones first. And they had gotten down to about 19 years old then. And they were delving into the 20s and were coming down. So since the war hadn't closed in November of that year, I'd probably been called out. Because they didn't know what to do with them for about a week or two. And then the harder it came, they were to disband them all by Christmas. Release them. So that wasn't much. I was in for a time in this last war too. I don't know why I was a little too old then. So they decided after a physical examination to release me.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: 1941, 1942 and 1943 I should say.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was university like when you went there?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Much smaller college. I think it was... I think a number of students in regular courses got down to exceeded 800. Washington State was probably twice that much. Or... Oh, it was the correspondence courses and short courses that they used to get in agriculture. I don't know if they still get them or not. Extension courses, no students. They totaled a couple hundred more. So at one time of the year in the winter, they called it the short eggs. Short eggs culture of course. That was the term for them. They're all short. There was a whole bunch of those. And then they had provided three months course. Special course for those. But you were taking... I took regular... AB course. I taught school for about 14 years after that. And I left it and went to Washington. And worked for the Bureau of the Division as they called it that time. Or office later on vital statistics. It was part of the Census Bureau when I left when I entered there. But a reorganization took place in the government there.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And they transferred it to the Public Health Service.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was DC?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Washington, DC.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You taught school before that? Did you teach around here?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I taught in California more than anywhere else. I did teach one year here in Idaho. I taught out in the Philippines for a while. And I was two years down at Clamor Falls Oregon. I went back to school at the University of California for a year after that. And then I was out in the Philippines for quite a while. And traveled a bit before I came back. Maybe ask you a little bit about what it was like being a student at the University in those days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much social life?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Plenty of social life, yes. Plenty of social life.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But what more should it sort of think than it had now?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, maybe some of the fraternities had a little drinking up there in private, but it was usually very private. They didn't let it get out. Just sort of kept quiet, but they talked about it as they went there. And there was a... And otherwise it was pretty quiet. What about the... Sometimes they had fights with other students. Of course, when you stayed from there at the game, that was sort of a silly thing. They did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All the big rivalry was there? Was it a big rivalry?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, it was quite a rivalry, yes. It turned out to be clashes between the Reuters. Yes, but... Oh, yes. Not very common, though. The presidents of the university at that time, that one over there in Washington State and Idaho, all they clamped down on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They understood each other as they was, didn't they?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And they were kind of... They even got kind of tough with students who tried starting any of that stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember President Lindley?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Lindley, yes. He was president from that year I graduated. But he left, I think, about that time, too. Lindley was one of those, just... I believe there should be no rioting and fighting and anything like that. There's no business around the university. Or vandalism or anything like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think very highly of him, do you remember?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, they all respected him. His son graduated the same year I did. He became a luthier man, he's K. Lindley. He wrote... Dating. Oh, that was loud, of course. I'll tell you, though, they had the dormitory. There was just one dormitory for girls. There wasn't big enough for that. And about four or five sororities, sororities, you know. Those girls were under pretty strict surveillance by the House Mothers and the Dean of Women and the Lute at the Ridenbaugh Hall, which is the only word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Dean French at that time?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Dean French.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Permeo Jane French. She had a reputation for strictness. Oh, yes, she was... The girls didn't like to be called up before her… yes… she was... I guess she... And of course there were always girls that lived out in the town, places too, you know, that lived in a hall to that. But she was Dean of all the women. You know, they didn't have a Dean of Men. That's one thing that most colleges even then had, but there were no Dean of Men. They weren't answerable to anybody, except for missing classes or anything else. Of course they might ruin their standing with their professors since they were... two lakhs in attending classes, but then it was up to them. It's a tricky double standard. Well, you talk about... double standard. Like, what was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: with relations between the sexes and that kind of thing?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, that is hard to say. I was thinking... Among university students, they go, I'm thinking about it, yes. You probably never know. Careful. Careful people there. Because... that sort of thing, if they were all against in... university, they wouldn't be tolerant to any person. These reputation was, as they say, tarnished by... sex out of affairs. And so... whatever happened... was kept between themselves, probably all. We just... never knew about it. I went on... The ROTC had an encampment one year when I was there. And... they had it up at Fort George right. And they mostly had a liberty at night. And... A lot of the boys went really wild up there. At that time... they had a chance to get out of Moscow... on their own for a while, waiting for the university campus. Well, there were a lot of... Nobody... no replications, whatever. They just don't... no report to that extent. They were out on their own. A million buyers remained. Anything like that. That's the way a... a commandant... could that car talk about those things, I'm sure. But that was... probably mostly like the body houses... Oh, yes, there was a lot of body houses in this building. Yes, that's what they went to. That's ridiculous. That's what they found, or... ones that were on their own. Freelances up there. That was kind of a freelance town for... women up there. The horses were not confined to the whorehouses. Too much... more private enterprise, and more... It was big enough to support that kind of thing, being a city. Yes. There wasn't really... such a thing... as a body house in Moscow. They were there, alright, in places. They were looming somewhere.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Rooming in a hotel, even. And when they appeared on the streets, they were like anybody else. But they could do their... solicitation very expertly. Probably even the hotel was unaware of it, or... maybe they looked at it as long as they behaved themselves. Maybe they didn't even know. I've often wondered about... you know, like the women that got... involved in that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are their backgrounds were? Where they came from?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: If they had... if they would tend to be... have grown up in poverty or... Well, I don't know. Drifted into it, I think, probably. I know... some of them started by... I think just... not as hoarse to begin with, but as... rather impulsive young girls as another. And then drifted into it for the house. I've kind of had the idea that... in those days, some things that I've read and stuff about... just that period of time... that some of the old Victorian standards were kind of starting to slip. And colleges... I know in the 20s, colleges got a little wild with the flappers and all that kind of stuff. Yes, I'll tell you. A fellow here, well, I went to school with him once... and... in college. And he went right on and got a PhD degree in physics. And became a physics teacher. He taught at University of Idaho, came back there and taught first, then he drifted on to Wisconsin. And during the War of War, he was summoned to Washington for their... for their group of scientists to work on things... on weapons. He told me that he was a professor here at Idaho, and he says, the things went... were changed completely after that war. He says, the boys were coming back. He says, some of them, two and... one and two and three and four years. And the time they had first started colleges. Some of them never got to college in the first place, starting at age 20, or 21 or 22. He says, and that time was the lowest ebb here on... the students had. He says, a lot of those boys, nothing really wrong with them. But his idea was, he said, they're too young for the experiences they had in the war. He said, this idea of sending the young men to war is... is tough on the young men. It has a deteriorating effect on them. They're too young to stand, it's... And that was about the time of the Second World War, he's talking about this. And he didn't like the idea of sending eight, eight and 19 year olds into war. He says, it's going to be... it's going to be rough on them. It's what I ever got into, was I told you that too young to carry that. And he sort of blamed that in the war, because we... went around to drafting those under 21. They registered them all. And a lot of them enlisted. It was 18 and 19 in that war. And 20. Went in by enlistment. In the wave of patriotism, I suppose you'd call it. But he was talking about the First World War. First World War, yes. And when he came back, he said that was the early 20s when they started coming back. He said... Of course they were probably quite a bit older than the girls too. They were older than the girls at that time. Girls, of course there were less girls during that war years than there were too. A lot of those dropped... dropped out and took positions in work forward. And earned considerably money when they went up. Well, the one thing about it, most of them didn't have much money up here. A large part of them were... were one and two and three years retarded on age, as you might say. But it's because they stayed out and worked.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You're talking about women or what?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Women and men both up here. Women were great to try to get a year or two in in college, in which they can draw a temporary teaching certificate that was only renewable after they... wasn't renewable after so many years unless they took more graduate... more college work. And they'd make their way that way, a lot of the way. If they got in a year, they could make it a temporary certificate in a year. And one a little bit longer, I think for five years, if they took it up. Two years. So they went out and they got teaching positions, and a lot of those were overaged girls that were coming back. Experienced teachers coming back to finish up. They didn't have very many choices in terms of the kind of courses, study, or career that we knew... No, they didn't. There was secretarial work, business,… and that was handled largely by business schools, so they had accounting there at the university, and some were taking that, and then teaching, and well, nursing, they always went to the hospital from high school. Well, there were some that took specializing in... home economics and dietitian and that. My nephew Keith, Bruce's brother, his wife, was a hospital dietitian before she came out of college. She worked at that for a time. Do you think that men, and this is a question I have because things have changed a lot, I think, are changing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that in those days that the men tended to look on the women, or the boys on the girls at the college there as being inferior to them, or being less than equal?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: You know what I mean. Well, I don't think that was so true. They may have thought that some things were for girls and some for me, and most of them recognized the girls, I think, as being intellectually equal. I'm a college man. Oh, there... probably that might not be true about the ordinary men, say, of 40 or 50. They'd been raised a generation before they were. Didn't have that idea, but a lot of those were pretty liberal on that point, too. Some people were not so keen on educating girls. They thought they were going to marry, because they said that was lost time. Because it was a loss. It wasn't economically beneficial.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think most men would have expected, and maybe the women, too, would have expected, that when they got married, she would give up her work and just become a homemaker?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: That was common. That was common, but a lot of them sometimes found they were glad that their wife had an occupation and a depression that she could work at that he wasn't prepared for. He was losing his job because of where his work was closing down, something like that, a plant to a man. No, they were married when they were teaching. With husbands. I know the wife of one of the professors up there took a job in Moss School High School, present at the university. All of her children were grown. She just had a couple. I talked with her once. She said, when I was taking a teaching certificate, we did our observation in Moss School High School in different things. I talked with her and told her why I was there. I talked with her a good deal. I knew her husband.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: She was the president?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Not the president, though. He was a history professor. History professor. I ended up, she told me that, just to sit at home board her… she had plenty of social life and reading without putting in full time to it. She took the job teaching. My idea of my remembrance of her, it's one of the easiest… ways of teaching she had that I had seen. She seemed to hold that class all over her hand, all interested. So she was an actual teacher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What meant you decided to become a teacher?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know. It was a little loss as to what I wanted to be. I wanted to go out and get a job immediately, and law and medicine sealed a long ways away. Well, I never theoretically thought of medicine. I never liked that kind of thing. But I did consider it a law, but then, sometimes a long time for a young lawyer to get started even then. The demand for lawyers wasn't so much in which way he suits. That's only, I multiplied extremely since I, in fact, as far as I remember, always suing for this and that. And several suits, we've seen on all kinds of questions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That way in the early days, too?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I wasn't near as much of it, I don't think, when I was a kid. You'd hear about suing for this and that, because then there wasn't much compared to what it is now. I think recourse to the law on several damages of any kind has increased tremendously in that time. They didn't think they'd do much things to suit and to fall down and hurt themselves when they're on somebody else's property. Now it is. I don't think anybody thought of that. You probably thought that they were too kind of slumsy. It was their fault. Right on. But it wasn't too difficult to put yourself in a position to be a teacher. No, you know, teachers are fairly well-in-demand. They were not so awfully high paid. And a lot of teachers, how they could enter something else. Science, a chemistry teacher would enter a chemical company, do something like that. So there was a constant change, and they opened up places for other people.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And sort of things like that happened all the time. And there was an increasing population. Both my immervation and excess of births, which ran pretty high, and then the increase of much better than 1 % yearly, natural growth 1 .5%, no doubt. And a steady stream of immigration, reaching a million a year sometimes. And the increase of a million a year from Europe. And that was, they were, there was no one that the schools kept increasing. The college enrollments, the high school enrollments, and the others. It was a great period of growth for the secondary schools about that time. They went through a fair, a minority of the population. It was, you know, at the time I started high school in 1912, after 13, which was, they didn't have much, they didn't have all their eighth grade graduates coming. They didn't have half of them. A lot of people thought that was for the bright ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there fewer country kids, proportionally, for talent kids?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, fewer proportion. Count kids were in the town, and they figured they just want to try.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think that was the reason it was easier access?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Easier access, and the fact that they didn't, and also they were out, they didn't, they wouldn't have a regular employment at age of entering high school with the country kids, what they had on their farms. So the parents were anxious for them to go. They probably thought their kid was average, you know. This was, my school was for intellectuals. How much time, you know, in the... I soon learned different when I went to high school. I could tell that the word intellectuals, he knew my age, from the schools.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much relation did the country people, like the Burnt Ridgers, have to the town, like the town of Troy? Do they have a lot to do with town?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, quite a bit, yes. Well, now, the thing is, a large part of them went to church there. And they had, let me go to the town populations who belonged to the same church. The church was there. And they went in, and they got acquainted with all the people they dealt with. And also they made friends with some of them around there. And a certain number of people in the tents in the town, after they went out and got jobs during the harvest, and so on, when they needed extra hands, went out and worked in the country. So there wasn't too much. There were lots of them. There was no strict division there between town people. For a town the smallest, Troy, it didn't matter too much. As compared to places like Moscow, where there was more difference. A group probably, it was largely because groups, the tlix, it was probably entirely town. But I don't think that would hold true in the country. They had mingled with the parts. In Moscow, I got the idea that there were a lot of people in the town that really felt they were better than country people. We'd call them hayseeds or... Oh, yes, they used those terms. I probably felt that. That was a way of calling each other and harassing each other. It was part of the time. It was something that was not too good-natured either. They used that kind of term. My name is term hayseed for a farmer. And all these other terms too. The loggers and the farmers kind of cooked jibes at each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But would you figure that there would be a little of that between, say, the people in Troy and the people in the ridges, as compared to places like Moscow?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't think it'd be all that different. I doubt so. It would remain that probably there was a larger group of fairly wealthy people in Moscow. They tended to cling together socially, that's about all. The rest had to deal with farmers a lot, a lot of business, and they had to trade with farmers. Which would keep that kind of thing down. Yes, a large part of their trade. Moscow had always had a lot of farmers around us. There was quite a little area there, and they loved the buying. Also, I was thinking whether, like in Troy, the kids even would identify better with country kids. Oh, yes, they would. There was no distinction. Maybe the kids knew each other. We had some kids come out there, and there were some kids that seemed to, I don't know, I guess maybe, for that matter, some of them had relatives in town. It'd be that, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel like a country kid when you went into the U .S.?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I don't know. I didn't feel particularly one way or the other. I just, I wanted to go very badly. I went up there. I guess maybe somebody regarded me as pretty much of a country kid. Maybe I was blissfully unaware. There must be a lot of country kids going to the U .S. in the ground state at that time. Oh, yes. A dozen of them were off the farm. Probably more came as many came off the farms than in the towns.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get involved in the fraternity, or did you stay in any?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, I never had any other fraternities. I stayed at the house with a group of boys once. They just organized, rung themselves for economy's sake, and rented a big house that was for rent to hold about 25 people. And hired a cook, and we all took care of our own rooms and cleaning, and that was it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your thinking about the fraternities then? Did you feel you're better not to be in one, or was it just not one?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I tell you, the economic cost, I lived on a fairly slim sentence. I realized it was a bit higher in the fraternities than where I was staying outside. And that was the deciding issue in that for me. I really didn't care all that much about the economic cost. Oh, maybe social advantages. I think they were overrated too. By the time I got through that, I figured it was over. By the time I reached my junior year, I thought fraternity life was overrated. Just what it gave the kids. And there was a lot of young men outside that were just as influential in politics as those in there. We were almost elected as every of our group down there. The president of my new field there, a small margin. A non-Greek letterman. Mostly Greek lettermen, if you want to know. There were just too many of them. There were quite a few fraternities for the population of it at that time. I don't know what the ratio is now or then either, so I guess I could say.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I suppose there were 400 men in regular attendance, and only half of them were fraternities.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that those kids that were not in fraternity would identify with each other better than what they were?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, maybe a little bit better, yes. Maybe a little bit better with each other than that. There was a lot of difference there. Some of my best friends were in fraternity then, and I knew the fellas probably down at the house who were non-fertility men better. I got acquainted with some that I had classes with and saw about, drilled this on the drill ground and all that. There wasn't any ill feeling, that is. I think the same thing was true of a lot of the fraternity men. A lot of them had their good friends in the non-Greek fraternity men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that, when you say that the economics, was there a significant cost difference to live in a fraternity?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, there was quite a bit. It was significant in terms of what resources they had then. Five dollars more then went to living costs, a whole lot more than it does now. You get a dollar worth something, you got a full scale meal, and a hotel restaurant for 35 cents. In terms of actual dollars, it probably wasn't so large, but in terms of what the dollar could buy, it was quite a bit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be like five dollars a month difference?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, more than that, I'd say it would cost ten dollars more. Maybe more than that. I know a lot of them spent a bit to go on to that. There are dues and the social life they are expected to attend. One of the fellows there, I roomed with him for a while. fraternity sort of solicited him and he decided to join after a while. I don't know, he told me he thought it would cost about ten to fifteen dollars more for him to live there. After he paid his initiation fees for the other interest, that sort of thing. I think that's a term he used. Anyway, it sounded pretty high to the fellows down there that were working. Another fellow I knew, they are here to see, two or three of them had stayed out one and two years working before they started school. They thought they were fixed to going through college. They weren't worried about to use it all up in one year. By joining. By joining fraternity or bring it down. They were budgeting their costs to go through. Lots of them got help from their parents of course, but not so many of them were entirely dependent on their parents. They worked summers and some of them worked jobs right there. They could get hold of part time jobs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: West dependent on their parents?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, they were financially less dependent. Then those that were in the fraternities. Oh yes, they were there. I would say that's definitely true. Those that were outside were less dependent. Then maybe. Sometimes I wondered about that, but I don't know. In some cases, individuals I just sort of wondered about. The times I had no way of knowing what their situation was. I imagine that you. One or two was, oh, there were one or two of the types they wouldn't invite into the fraternity. They were some that were kind of oddball and manner. I remember one, he was quite big in some things. They had the sloppiest dress and all that. And it didn't fit well into any social group. He antagonized people. They thought he was odd. He studied accounting I think, eventually. Here is a sort of speed demon on reading fast and getting heated.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you seen those things advertised as speed reading now?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I think he was a speed reader before that thing was invented. Also, a speedster in mathematics for quickness. They tested him against an adding machine with an experienced person at the controls of an adding machine. He could beat it on figures. Adding a column of figures faster than she could put them in. He'd come up with the right answer before she would. But he didn't want to be. Well, no. He was odd in many ways. He tried teaching school afterward and he wasn't much of a success at that. He couldn't hold a job. He looked around at various things. He was promoting land deals, mining deals, things like that. He never settled. I don't think it was a regular job all his life. I saw him a few times after he was in college. And he was odd. I didn't dislike him at all. I talked to him a good deal and the fact is he came from Latah County. Here, one of these backwoods farms beyond dairy. I think he probably has relative solidity in this guy. I've heard him because I heard he was a wizard of figures. Yeah, shorty-working. Oscar-working. Oscar, I think it was his name.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the young people from this area kind of keep track of each other at college?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, yes. If you saw somebody from here, we generally would. But sometimes or other, you know there was a lot of piling up by what course they came. Law students were law students. Engineers with engineers and so on. Regardless of whether they were fraternity or non-fraternity. That's the way a lot of them found their friends there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What subject did you specialize in?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I took a major in history and minors and English and mathematics.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why history?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I liked it. I don't know whether it was a good job for Santa or not. After I got into high school teaching, I was teaching mostly mathematics. In fact, I think I liked it better than teaching history. It's more definite. You could tell how a student was doing. A lot easier. Where he was having trouble. In teaching mathematics and in history or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel when you were studying history that there was much tie-in between the pioneering that went on out here and the whole idea of westward expansion?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, no. You know, we never considered ourselves pioneers. We considered the pioneers those that had arrived about 30, 40 years old. Years before. We could say we passed that stage. We thought we were right up to date. People looking back on it, knowing how we lived without electricity, without automobiles and all that, they probably thought we were. People didn't have those decisions. They were everywhere else. It was just something that came along. No, it wasn't until just about World War I, or a little before, that automobiles began to become commoner and commoner. About 1910 and 11. The tempo increased. One of the things was the poor quality of the engines that they had at that time. You'd see people stalled along the roads. Something had gone wrong with them. They had to have it all off the center of the canyon. A farmer with a team of horses to haul it in to town.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: When cars became dependable enough so you'd go someplace and come back again, fair chance of not having to have a breakdown. Then they'd be selling something. That was one of the qualities of that. They had a little too hot, or tires, all that. Of course, the roads had a lot to do with the bad tires. They had very little pavement down on it. They tossed gravel on some of your roads. That was the way of putting a solid base in and pulling down the mud. They made it rough, a lot of them were stoning you roads. The grades had to be laid out, and there was an awful lot of these straight uphill and straight downhill stuff. So it was rough on the cars to standards. Any kind of a car would be roughened by the kind of roads they had. It was poorer cars. People thought those things they'd better keep to the cities where they could travel on a smooth road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How difficult did travel get in the winter from where you lived?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, where we lived, we were six miles out of town. We always made it all right. Sometimes you'd get too much snowfall to go on in. We'd break it off very hard roads with our horses. Sometimes after we had the cars, we used the cars. The cars were something that hard to get through. When the horses, they went back to us. Shucks, I was up here. Once I came back, I'd been teaching California. I came up here. Christmas. There was a darn much snow that piled up about six feet. People were coming in, but it had to further places. They had to abandon their car travel, and they were putting horses through Memphis. Oh, that was around 1935. There were still lots of horses, horses we used at that time. They were on the way out. We were more and more recognized for our new horses each year until they disappeared.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In talking about social life at the university, I was wondering, what were the kind of things that you did for social life?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: It was mostly campus dances. Oh, yes, they were. They were generally held in the gymnasiums there. They had the regular ones. Otherwise, students would often organize picnics for bunches. I know, I went on to sub up to Moscow Mountains, a few of them. We were horse-drawn that day. We had a haywax. A whole bunch of us could get in and roll on up. Then, I don't know, there was always a group of churchgoers that lived their social life in the churches. It was a mixed group of people, town and college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you generally live in an apartment?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I lived in a rooming house most of the time there. Generally, there were rooms and meals in the same house. There were a bunch of boys that all go to the university. I think there were one or two sometimes that weren't. I know one high school boy who was living away from home. Stayed there one year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father wasn't very strong on the churches, was he?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, he wasn't. He believed firmly in the Lutheran interpretation. He thought that the churches were too commercialized. They were defying too far from what was recommended. He was pretty much a fundamentalist, you might say. He thought that they made churches too much of a question of building and money and all that. It wasn't occupied their time too much. He was not too strong for churches. Though he supported that church in Nora pretty regularly. The old Nora church up in Bear Creek, which is what they called, it was called, well determined, with mission friends translated, with Swedish free church. It was Lutheran doctrine, frankly, but it had departed from it. That church lasted up there for quite a while, up there in Nora. They put out an awful lot of the missionaries in the field. Most of the ministers that I remember out there had served in Alaska, among the Eskimos, with one time or another. They had served there or went up there to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think would be the difference in the kind of departure from doctrine that your father would see?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, he didn't like the formalism of the church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: He was a better side of the church where the congregation just got together and listened to a fairly learned man talk on the Bible and his relation to ordinary living. More than he, he didn't, I always thought that he belonged in the disciples of Christ, that church, commonly known as the Christian church. He was more like that, I think he was outlooked at any other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would that be more of a revival kind of church?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, it isn't. It's a creedless church. You don't care what you believe. Believe what you like. All they are concerned with is how a person lives his life and what he can do to live it like they say a Christian should.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your father was more interested in the idea of religion as an ethical force in a person's life?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, absolutely… he sounds like a real, like a free thinker, I mean an independent thinker. Well, he was. He had ideas on most subjects to become in control and more often disagreed with people than could agree.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that that encouraged, that his philosophy encouraged his children to think for themselves?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I think so. Oh, I suppose it did. I was not particularly vibrant, but I think it did. He said people ought to read and read and think for themselves. He was a great reader all his life. He spent lots of time. He subscribed to various newspapers, sometimes several of them, I was around just to read what they had to say in their editorials. All that. And then he read various books sometimes. Some of those books I just wondered why he got them. He said he was learning English when he came over here. As soon as he could speak it a little while, he got books and read them and learned how the language was used that way. And he wasn't particular what kind of book he got hold of, though he got hold of some that he knew were good. What the subject matter was, it was largely history and religion and a few famous classics. I know one of the books I read early because it was in our library was Sinkuic's Covetus. I read it before I could fully understand it. Don Quixote says things like that. Then there was a book of English poets, as I remember. One of the great big books of English poets who even bought those things at different times.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would he involve your mother in those discussions too?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, not much. She wasn't too much interested in that. She was much more interested in the whole. The well-being of the children, things like that, what to do next.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that he was less practical than some guys?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Probably he was. He wasn't the best of farmers, I don't think. I think Brother Walter learned how to farm faster than he did, under the conditions in which we farmed here. He had been raised on a farm in Sweden, but evidently they were these small farms. A large part of the time was spent in a large part of the time was woodsmen, forests nearby there. I saw farms in Sweden, he might have small farms, but when I first went back there, they had a whole lot better machinery than he ever saw. Machinery was invented after he came here. It wouldn't have been the same thing. If you farm a ten acre plot or a sixteen acre plot, and raise some rye on it and haul it to the mill and have a ground, get a meal and bring the flower home, like they used to do then, that's a lot different than raising the cell for the support economics. They got their ready money for working in the woods there.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Different kind of life and tiredness. The one thing about that that reminds me of is the fuel system I thought was still pretty strong there. When your father left, I mean it was some big landowners. Well, there were some big landowners, but I think comparatively speaking,… there weren't so many. It seemed to be, what they called a big landowner would never have been called a big landowner. It's all matter of comparison. Well, I'm talking about these early days when your father left. Yes, I mean that. They spoke of a person who had a big tract of land. Oh heck, because he didn't have a hundred acres in cultivation on it. This was smaller, he was in his comparative, and it was all comparative. But of course there were large tracts that doddily held in some places of Sweden from ancient times. There was a bandage to hang onto that land.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That meant that you kids didn't go?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: We didn't go regular in the church, no. I think they were probably the guy that was a church that was a guard that was pretty much a he at that time. That never bothered us really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you go like a nut?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I remember when the Kelvird family came from Missouri. They were Swedish, but they had lived in a non-Swedish community, and probably they were reared Lutheran. Mrs. Anderson was one of them. She was about two years old. But after they got here, I think Mrs. Kelvird, and realized there was a Lutheran church in Troy, she immediately wanted to go. So they joined that church and went there. But I believe one of the boys from the year had a church in that community in Missouri where they lived. They were attentive before that. Of course, the old sport was only about ten when they came. That's the way they were influenced by the fact that they had landed in the Swedish community. The old folks were Swedish, so they just reverted back. They had gotten along very well with that church, apparently, for the interval, without being missing much.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you be inclined to go on Christmas or K-12?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, yes. Yes, we would. In fact,… we went, certainly always went up to that old Swedish custom of morning service on Christmas, starting at 6 o 'clock. That was Christmas, the one that Dad insisted on going to, because I think it was reminiscent to actually the old days, and Sweden sort of made a... It felt that that was something that belonged. I don't know. They're all Easter, the same thing. I don't know. I wonder if this Luther Church practices the early morning service anymore. I don't know whether it does or not. The one other thing I wanted to ask you about was how close you feel that the community was on Brooklyn Ridge in the early days. Oh, I don't know. I think they felt friendly for each other, most people. They were neighbors, but I don't know if there was any great closeness, except two special families felt close to each other. Others didn't. Oh, Kelberg and his brother-in-law lived on the adjoining place just about north of us. Those kids consorted together a great deal. The Ruhr family were cousins with about three families on the ridge, related, you know. We got to the northern part of the ridge, and we got to Lawrence Johnson's. Oh, he was the mother of the Dilleen Joden. There are still Dilleen's around here. Lawrence was Clarence's father. He was sister to the brother of Izzy Dilleen.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And a cousin to the grandfather of the Larson children of the 20th year. And I guess they were cousins or second cousins to the Andersons that lived up there. They were about quite a group there of peoples that were related. One came, and he told about it, and he'd land each other, and got them to move in, and he'd find a piece of land for them. Now, Ruhr Dilleen, who lived up there, was a rather distant reality of my father, but he'd grown up, and he'd been sweet, exactly. They'd gone to the same schools at the same age, and they kept track of each other in this country. And Dad was more or less responsible for him being here. He'd bought that place out there, and he saw that land, and what they were doing with it. He wrote to see what he'd done. Then he told him the land seemed to be what looked like good land to him, seemed to be going off as cheap out here. And Ruhr Dilleen got immediately interested. And when the chance to buy came up here, he sent a chunk of money to him and put down his auction until he got out of here and bought.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: They were our closest friends, the Ruhr Dines, mostly because, simply because Dad and the Ruhr Dines were old friends rather than, oh, what the word, they were second cousins or third cousins or something like that. Sounds like kinship played an important part, though. Kinship played an important part, there. Now, the Swansons came later. Frank Swansons came just about with his family about the same time. He's Alvin Swansons' father. And I think two of his children were born after the King's word reached. But then just two of his brothers came down there, and a wife of yours were unmarried, young men. One of them married a Dilleen girl and another married Anderson girl. Got intermarried one generation, but people already settled there. Ed continued to live out there. He's got to send us right now that Austin Swansons and Richie and Eddie were his grandsons. George, he lived somewhere else. He went up to around Spokane or Coeur d 'Alene, settled up there. Sounds like kinship was maybe the single most important part of this. Well, probably was. And I think the kinship sort of reached together there on a lot of that. People got related from living next to each other. Yes, they did. A lot of these people are related out here in different degrees of cousinship from those early marriages. I imagine quite a few of those people on Perk Ridge who would argue best about second cousin, third cousin, and fourth cousins. Relationships living right here in the Laitaw counties. And I know it had some effect because Calvary came mostly because of the Alders that settled there. And Mrs. Alder was Calvary's sister and she was very close to her family. She was glad to have Calvary come out. And later she had two brothers. Mrs. Calvary had two brothers. One of them came out here as a young man. I just probably earned the attention and married somebody here and settled. And then they induced another one to come from Oklahoma. And he came. Both the Calvarys and the Alders had come to visit Oklahoma before that. I don't know. I know Alder was what they called an 89er. He was in the rush down there. I heard a number of people had stopped in Ashtabula at some point. Ashtabula, Ohio, yes. A lot of them went in there and established themselves. Some came out here and some came later. But I think now Clarence Johnson's father, or maybe it was his mother, that they were established there for a while and then came on out.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: And then he came back and he was married to Mrs. Carion. These kinship ties, I take it, though, meant kind of that you were oftentimes like with your father and Mr. Redeen, that they were together in the old country or back there and they were kin back there. They had that kind of tie. There was a kind of tie there in kinship. I didn't think about it, but I learned that the same thing was true over on American range. Those Cheney families moved in. And I think some of their wives are related to some of the other people there. I know the Harlan family and the West. They were related. The Duns and Cunninghams were sisters, some of the Griskel men. There was the old patriarch history. He was older. His sons were older than my father. But he came out here too. He would be an old man. He died long before my time. Do you think there's a kind of natural tendency to put more trust or rely more on people you're related to than people that you just...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...friends with in those days?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I think possibly there was. I think they clung to their own quite a bit and trusted much more. I think that's true. A lot of those people first came... Old and birderage were all from... Well, the red-lated ones were all from one province there. A single province there, where all these Andersons and the Leads came from.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which province?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: But also the Andersons.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, which province?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: The province was called Vesterjötland. West Gotland, a translator to use. But a lot of those others were. I know Westbury's were from there. And I guess the Kelberts from there, the Swans, but Alder, he himself was... He was from some other section, I know. It just seemed that an awful lot of the people that... ...that landed there was from that province. Well, you think that the people who were not Swedish, who lived on Burnt Ridge, when you were growing up, there were some of those. Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that they just weren't as much of a part of the community?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I think possibly they were. They did feel that way. I don't think they deliberately made them feel that way, but of course they belonged to... ...didn't belong to that church. I know... Sanders, I don't think he was the main friendly to us, despite the fact that old man Sanders had circuit peculiarities. His main peculiarities came from Jags and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do? Go on in town?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Drinking.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Drinking?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: He had... He... Every so often he had a hard drinking bout. That made him... That made some of the more sobers... It's good as... ...people not... Yeah. Of course, they weren't all... all... teetoterers, by any means. Most of them. The large part of it, we're accustomed to drinking. So some weren't. As far as I know, I could probably name the ones, but maybe anything particular. But I know some of those. We had different ones there, but so many of those didn't stay long enough to get acquainted. Sanders family, they're party friends with... with the people of our church. But now, of course, Joe Porter, he settled down there. He was the man that I said owned the thrash machine. But he was the like... fellow that liked to act tough. He carried his revolver on the lady's side and... ...with all his appearance, trying to bully somebody. Things happened to him. And after a while, he would get pushed back. And that sort of set people apart from him because he was... He wasn't acting particularly friendly and liked to be any friends. But these kids were nice kids, my buddy. Two girls and a boy. They got along alright with the kids in school. And it wasn't...

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: ...too bad. But I don't know. They may have felt a little like outsiders. Because they were... They were a part of the community like the others were. I don't think they... very many people invited Porter into their homes, he and his wife. Well, of course, there was a thing, yeah. His wife was a part Indian. About half a quarter. But those children... ...didn't show much ending. And I think they all married white, or something. They probably disappeared into white race from the Indians.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that Burt Wood Schoolhouse used for community get-togethers?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes. That's where... it was really a community get-together. There's some speakers, you know, a church, missionary or such, of the denomination or any kind. If you were invited there to speak to him, he could go there. If he wanted a politician, wanted an election time, wanted it, he could call me there in the evenings. A farm-of-year organizer I heard speak there once. He was in there. And they went... they went there. And they used to have their old parties at night. About Friday nights in the winter time, they'd always have some kind of a party there. Social, box social, or just get-together with a little program of singing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they play party games there?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Party games, yeah. Well, I tell you, in the schoolroom, they couldn't really play that. There was a... it was not a room, it was filled with seats. They played those outdoors in the summer. It wasn't the weather, it was good. They played those locked. I was thinking when the adults got together. Oh, yes, I mean the adults there. They wouldn't... to play it inside that schoolhouse wasn't space enough. Because it wasn't that big a schoolhouse. It probably all filled with seats, mostly. But I remember those party games used to be played a lot, some indoors and some outdoors, and houses around town. Oh, I remember years ago as a small kid, they had one down at our house. I thought there was a terrific lot of people there, maybe they worked more than 15. Before I was a school age.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they have dancing on Burnt Ridge, or was that frowned on?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I tell you, a lot of those church members frowned on their children dancing. But nevertheless, they weren't going to town, to the community dances in town. They were pretty regular too, public dances. And I guess they got used to it because eventually some of the police would dance there. There were quite a few places there, Coles there, that opened it up to dancing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Even on Burnt Ridge? This was later?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, I don't know, a little later. Maybe I was 17, 18 years old. Shelbert let him have a barn dance so he built his big barn before they put in the hay. He didn't care one way or the other. Shelbert, I don't think was a religious man, he was probably good for his kids to go to church, and it was a comfort to his wife. Great old character he's old way to, Mr. Colberg.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Oh, he was a joker. He was very sarcastic and pretty sure about things too. But he was very vocal. Nobody scared him. Oh, he wasn't a powerful man, he just wasn't afraid of anybody. Regardless of how big he was, he let him speak up and speak his feet. I liked Mr. Colberg.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were he and your father very good friends?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: You were always good friends, yes. My father got disgusted he wasn't a while because he was a great teller of tall stories. You ever knew whether he was just laying it all in a while or whether he was actually a good incident that usually happened. He was a good deal older than his wife and very nice. He was about the age of my father. And made him about 35 years or so older than his oldest child probably. So he was over quite a while in this country. He was in Wyoming for a while. He rode as a cowboy on wrenches out there for a number of years, things like that. He said his introduction, I remember that, he told his introduction to Cheyenne. The railroad was coming, moving on the railroad there into Cheyenne, Union Pacific. As they came there, they saw a man hanging on a telescope crack hole. He counted seven of them. Then they had been hanged in telephone holes, they had been on a lynching pipe. The introduction to Cheyenne when he came in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now is that supposed to be true or not?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, I don't know. I just wonder if he was laying it on. He never could tell. He could have been proved. He swears it was true, but there was no one to investigate whether that happened or not. He probably didn't care. One more thing about these dances, or these get-togethers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Usually if you had a get-together at somebody's home, would it be for dancing or for play-party?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, play-party, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd play party games and that kind of thing? I know they had, what was it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Actually, there was only one or two places. But they did a dancing to about anything. One was a big square house up there. Things had taken down long since. People there didn't object to dancing and they had a good sized part where people could dance around there. They couldn't entertain a whole lot of people, but they didn't mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they Swedish? His family?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I don't know what they were. Several families lived there at one time or another. I can't even recall their name now, but I know they went there. They said they could dance there. They danced to somebody who was invited there for dinner. It wouldn't be usually specially given for young people to want to dance. They came there and the people wanted to dance, and I think they would dance. But I seem to remember, oh, I do remember that there was one place. There was a house that was vacant a good deal. It was right through several hands. It had been purchased by someone who lived elsewhere. He left them into that house to use that. They had a good sized living room. They dived into the house. If you didn't mind crowding a little, they could get a dozen couples, maybe on the floor at once. Probably less actually there. They danced there some. I know it was one of the boys that I went to school with. He always lived over in the diamonds six years old. He was running a farm at that time. His mother still owned it. She was widowed. He liked to dance. He had been away attending college for a little while. He liked dancing, so he let him in. They had it there. He lived at the home place there. He and his sister, his sister was a much younger neighbor. She kept house for him one year when he was farming there. There they danced. It wasn't the old folks, either case. It was the younger generation going up.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So would it be fair to say that the older generation didn't approve of it?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
