words: They didn't really try to stop the kids after they were grown, or near grown. I think they realized there was a few. Just used kind of common sense on that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about younger generation drinking? What about the boys having a drink or two?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I tell you, I think the boys were less inclined for a little sip down and in than some of their fathers were. Some weren't. I know... I know there were a couple of bad habits. They'd like a drink once in a while. They went and stood around the bars downtown. They might take a bottle home and have it there. Get a neighborhood drink if you came in. That was about all. Some were dead against it. Now it was odd about one family, I know, out there. All the boys eventually became more or less of drinkers. None of them. None of them were real toppers. But occasionally, the spread was on social drinkers. But their father was dead against it. He wouldn't touch it. One of the reasons was that he had a hard drinking father. And he thought it made things pretty tough on the kids. So he stayed away from it entirely. But his son sort of... They thought their father couldn't get any fun all of life, I guess.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I remember them. He was... they weren't. They were never, as I understood, nuisances on the count of alcohol, anywhere, at any time. I don't know. I think... Well, a few of the boys drank a little there. I don't know. I remember a brother Paul's generation. He liked a little drink when I was in. One or two of them could get toot it up a little, sometimes. Right. In the Tilleen family, they're jawned. A jawn could drink... would always like a little drink. And sometimes you drank too much, I said. I never saw him that way. I didn't think he was. But he... But I was still a kid when he grew up. He was about 15, 16 years older than I was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the younger generation was less religious than anyone else?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Definitely religious. Definitely, yes. A lot of them just stopped on the street. When they grew up, the parents weren't big. Hi.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How are you?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, I... Things just grow with the... Grow with the kind of life we had in this country. I think it was less... there was more doubt on religious matters. Growing up through that period. They used to accept it, and then there was less and less of that. And I think there's maybe a revolt against the strictures they put on them. Well, going to church all after, they didn't feel like going to church all the way. And maybe all that being against dancing so much and all that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of them came here to be independent in their thoughts there, didn't we?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: It didn't include church. I'd say that David Swanson was one of the earliest free thinkers I knew to express himself on that. And I took Wayne with me as I went, and I dropped... He's going to Anderson's for dinner at San Antonio.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You want a cup of coffee?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: We go down to the grave, and then it makes more earth, and more life comes from that. That's all. Most of them expressed themselves very much on religion that I remember as the young perch ragers. Oh, and he called last night. A lot of them, after they got married, saw that their children went back to church. Oh. Because they hadn't been very good church lovers at the meantime. So it's hard to linger, it's all right. It'll come to you. The time that they found Hange was murdered, nobody knows whether he committed suicide or who he was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which murder in Troy thing?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, the first one I remember. I was just a small kid then. But it made an impression on me, because though I'd heard of murders and knew what they were, I thought they happened. A while of being people living a long ways from the kind of people I knew. But the marshal was shot down in Troy, and I thought he went down to arrest. Most people thought that there was more or less a case of self-defense. They thought he went down there and tried forcing him. Forced him to resist and have an excuse for killing him. That's what a lot of people thought. It was whole different ways.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You haven't accounted for that?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: There's a little account of that in that book on Texas, on American Ridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And Risco?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: I've heard that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was Hayes pretty unpopular?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Yes, he was unpopular. Yes. Except with his cronies. But he didn't have too many of them. Well, I don't know. My father despised Hange. He said he was crooked. He and his boys, Henry and Harry, worked a game on the people there. Harry would pick a fight in the saloon, and the old man would come in and arrest the other fellow for assault and battery. And bring him to jail and probably club him over the head. And then when he came up to court, he'd bring a couple of things. He had a knife, maybe a watch, and he'd take it off. In the pocketbook, there was maybe a dollar left in it. Well, that must have been all he had. And the fellow would swear that he had more in there. And it happens irregularly. He thought that was a game. He was making money that way by arresting people and lifting their wallets.

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Could have been, too. It could have been. He didn't last too long in this community after that. Two Hange boys pulled out fairly soon, I think. I think there was another area. She had another item in there that I always heard differently. And that is, it said that the pain had slide. It said that he was going to get the marshal. But I heard it the other way around. The marshal said that he was going to get Hange. Or he was going to get pain. So that's two stories there. I don't know which is right. I had heard, one or two people said to me that after the killing, that there were a number of people that wanted to lynch pain slide. Yes, yes. There was two, the marshal's two sons, Henry and Harry. They were twins. I don't know if Bill was here. He was an older son or not at that time. But that leads up to the other contradiction, from what I had heard, the way I heard the story. He said that he was surrendered and he was brought to Moscow. Well, it didn't say really how he surrendered. But I heard he just took his rifle and walked across the railroad tracks and up into the American-made side there, where there was plenty of woods at that time. Then he crossed over to the Moscow Road and sat down at the side of the road and waited for them. He figured the marshal would be a telephone for, or a telegraph for you. I think he didn't have much of a telephone at that time. And he'd come down for him and then the sheriff came down from there. He walked out and surrendered himself. And the sheriff just turned his team around and drove back to Moscow, notified him of paying trolley, paid slimes in custody. He wasn't taking a chance on those people. That's the way you heard it. That's the way I heard it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And there was another murder, too?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: Well, Clem, a fellow that was, oh, I don't know, he had a, well, his wife ran a boarding house and I don't know, he was a hard drinker. He was found in an alley down there, back at a little restaurant, or near the, well, not co-occur about the, the alleyway ran past a restaurant. And he was found in a morning, I think, or late at night or something, dead there. And he looked as if he had his head banged in. No one was arrested for that. He didn't know who had done it. He might have been even in a fight, somebody had knocked him down or hit his head or something like that. But he was, he was probably very drunk. He was because he had a habit, a daily habit of it. I had heard something about their suspicion that there was this black guy that were on a restaurant in Troy that had done that. Oh, he had done that. Somebody started that. But as I understood it, there wasn't a Niota restaurant of evidence that this guy had done it. But he was, the last they do of him, he was tending to his affairs in the restaurant until he closed up and went home.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So nothing ever happened to him?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: No, nothing ever happened to him. There was nothing nice to us, to the rest of you want. There was a third of it down here, and that seemed to be a senseless murder. I never understood exactly why, why it came to pass. They said there were, there was a witness to that. And the story given was that those two men seemed to be quarreling, but not very hard. And somebody said he'd give him, he'd give him something. And he took a rammed a knife into him. And why he was suddenly enraged. But anyway, he was arrested and brought to trial. I never did know just exactly what kind of a sentence he got. Cain's sly got life sentence, but he had spent more than six and a eighth years there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now, is he the forerunner of the other slies around Troy?

speaker: Gustav (Bud) Carlson
words: He was a brother of old Jake's sly and Ike's sly, who were the other sly.
